Using GPT-2 style models trained on perturbed "impossible" variants of English, we measure sensitivity to grammaticality using BLiMP minimal pairs, finding that model performance exhibits only gradual degradation, mediated by the language's information locality. In contrast, these models exhibited pronounced failures in generation, producing substantially fewer high-quality sentences at longer lengths. Together, these results suggest generative deficiency and transmission failures as a plausible linking hypothesis between language model behaviour and non-attestation of impossible languages.

</details>


### [87] [Test-Time Verification for Text-to-SQL via Outcome Reward Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30851)
*Mattia Tritto,Giuseppe Farano,Dario Di Palma,Gaetano Rossiello,Fedelucio Narducci,Dharmashankar Subramanian,Tommaso Di Noia*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents GradeSQL, a framework for using Outcome Reward Models (ORMs) to improve the reliability of large language models (LLMs) in Text-to-SQL tasks, showing improvements over heuristic methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of improving the reliability of LLM outputs in structured reasoning tasks like Text-to-SQL by moving beyond heuristic test-time verification approaches.

Method: The authors propose training Outcome Reward Models (ORMs) for semantic scoring using automated candidate generation and execution-based labeling, integrating these models into a verification pipeline for selecting the best output in Text-to-SQL tasks.

Result: ORM-based selection outperformed traditional heuristic methods such as execution-based Best-of-N and Majority Voting, achieving up to +4.33% gains on the BIRD benchmark and +2.10% on Spider, with higher effectiveness on complex queries.

Conclusion: ORMs provide a scalable, effective, and more reliable alternative to heuristic test-time selection strategies for Text-to-SQL, as demonstrated by consistent improvements on multiple benchmarks.

Abstract: Improving the reliability of large language models (LLMs) at inference time is a central challenge in structured reasoning tasks such as Text-to-SQL. Common test-time inference strategies, including Best-of-N sampling and Majority Voting, rely on heuristic signals such as execution success or output frequency, which provide limited semantic discrimination across candidate outputs. In this work, we study Outcome Reward Models (ORMs) as learned semantic scoring functions for test-time verification in Text-to-SQL. While ORMs have been previously explored for test-time scaling and alignment, their application to structured query generation remains underexplored. We introduce GradeSQL, a scalable framework for training task-specific ORMs via automated candidate generation and execution-based labeling, enabling verifier training without manual annotation. We integrate ORMs into a verification-driven Best-of-N pipeline and evaluate our approach on the BIRD and Spider benchmarks across multiple open-source LLM families. ORM-based selection consistently outperforms execution-based Best-of-N and Majority Voting, with gains of up to +4.33% on BIRD and +2.10% on Spider. We further show that ORMs scale effectively with larger candidate sets and yield stronger improvements on complex queries. Overall, our results demonstrate that ORM-based verification provides a simple, effective, and scalable alternative to heuristic test-time selection strategies for Text-to-SQL. Code datasets and models are publicly available.

</details>


### [88] [Multilingual Polarization Detection Using Transformer-Based Models with Class Weighting and Threshold Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30857)
*Aaron Bundi Anampiu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents a transformer-based method for detecting online polarization in English and Swahili, participating in SemEval-2026 Task 9, achieving competitive F1 scores for three subtasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the challenge of detecting multilingual, multicultural, and multievent online polarization, which is increasingly critical in mitigating divisive content on digital platforms.

Method: The authors use RoBERTa-base for English and AfroXLMR-base for Swahili with class-weighted loss functions and per-label threshold tuning to handle severe label imbalances in multi-label classification tasks.

Result: The approach achieved F1 macro scores of 0.7901 (English) and 0.7910 (Swahili) for binary detection, 0.4615 (English) and 0.4808 (Swahili) for polarization type classification, and 0.4791 (English) and 0.5830 (Swahili) for manifestation identification.

Conclusion: The study shows that transformer-based methods with appropriate adjustments for handling class imbalance can effectively detect online polarization, though improvements are needed in areas like dehumanization and empathy detection.

Abstract: This paper describes our submission to SemEval-2026 Task 9 on detecting multilingual, multicultural, and multievent online polarization. We address all three subtasks: binary polarization detection, polarization type classification, and manifestation identification for English and Swahili. Our approach leverages transformer-based models (RoBERTa-base for English, AfroXLMR-base for Swahili) with class-weighted loss functions to address severe label imbalance and per-label threshold tuning to optimize multi-label classification. On the test set, we achieve F1 macro scores of 0.7901 (English) and 0.7910 (Swahili) for Subtask 1, 0.4615 (English) and 0.4808 (Swahili) for Subtask 2 and 0.4791 (English) and 0.5830 (Swahili) for Subtask 3, which give competitive performance on the leaderboard, demonstrating the effectiveness of our methods for handling imbalanced multi-label polarization detection. Our error analysis reveals that models struggle with dehumanization detection and lack of empathy.

</details>


### [89] [Training Therapeutic Judges and Multi-Agent Systems for Human-Aligned Mental Health Support](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30887)
*Mizanur Rahman,Abeer Badawi,Elahe Rahimi,Laleh Seyyed-Kalantari,Frank Rudzicz,Enamul Hoque,Elham Dolatabadi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper discusses improving mental health language model (LLM) support by introducing TheraJudge and TheraAgent for multi-dimensional evaluation and generation refinement, achieving better therapeutic quality through actionable feedback.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the gap in therapeutic quality of language models for mental health by transitioning from passive evaluation metrics to actionable control signals.

Method: The method involves a two-stage framework: (1) Developing TheraJudge, a therapeutic response evaluator trained on human annotations for reliable judgments across psychological dimensions; and (2) Designing TheraAgent, which refines responses using roles (Critic, Coach, Therapist) based on evaluations from TheraJudge.

Result: TheraJudge achieves high agreement with clinician ratings (ICC = 0.87–0.95) on dimensions like Safety and Empathy, outperforming baselines. TheraAgent improves therapeutic response quality by +0.43, with notable recovery rates for low-quality responses (+2.45 points improvement and 94% recovery).

Conclusion: Aligning LLMs for mental health support requires acting on human-aligned evaluation rather than solely enhancing generation. The proposed framework proves effective in improving therapeutic outputs.

Abstract: Large language models show promise for mental health support, yet therapeutic quality improves only when evaluation functions as an actionable control signal rather than a passive metric. We introduce a framework that formulates therapeutic response generation as a decision-refinement problem driven by multi-dimensional, human-aligned evaluation. In Stage I, we introduce TheraJudge, an open-source therapeutic evaluator trained via preference-based optimization on human-annotated data to produce reliable judgments across 7 psychological dimensions. In Stage II, we introduce TheraAgent, which operationalizes TheraJudge's evaluations through a coordinated refinement process with specialized Critic, Coach, and Therapist roles that translate evaluative signals into targeted response revisions. Empirically, TheraJudge achieves strong agreement with clinician ratings, with intraclass correlation coefficients (ICC = 0.87-0.95), surpassing supervised baselines and strong closed-source judges, particularly on critical dimensions such as Safety, Relevance, and Empathy. Acting on these evaluations, TheraAgent yields a +0.43 improvement in human-rated therapeutic quality (on a 5-point scale) under blind evaluation, with 96\% clinician inter-rater reliability. Low-quality responses ($\leq 3$) improve by +2.45 points with a 94\% recovery rate, demonstrating targeted correction of unsafe outputs. Overall, our results indicate that effective alignment of mental-health LLMs stems from acting on human-aligned evaluation, rather than relying solely on stronger generation. We release code at https://github.com/vis-nlp/TheraAlign.

</details>


### [90] [Beyond Clean Text: Evaluating Encoder and Decoder Robustness for Bangla Event Detection in Noisy Text](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30914)
*Tanvir Ahmed Sijan,S. M Golam Rifat,Nayeemul Islam,Md. Musfique Anwar*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces a benchmark for Bangla event detection covering clean news text, ASR transcripts, and orthographically corrupted text. It evaluates encoder-only and instruction-tuned decoder-only models, finding that encoder models excel on clean text but degrade under noise, whereas decoder models are more robust.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Event detection systems are often tested on clean text, ignoring their robustness in noisy, real-world scenarios, especially for low-resource languages like Bangla.

Method: A Bangla-specific event ontology and a benchmark of 9,979 annotated sentences spanning different noise types were developed. Encoder-only models (BanglaBERT, XLM-R) and instruction-tuned decoder-only LLMs (Llama 3, Gemma 3) were systematically evaluated.

Result: Encoder-only models perform better on clean text but struggle with noise, while decoder-only LLMs exhibit higher robustness to noisy conditions, especially for corrupted event triggers. Embedding annotation guidelines helps noisy text performance but has mixed effects on degradation.

Conclusion: Scaling decoder-only models improves robustness, and combined clean/noisy data training significantly benefits encoder models, narrowing the robustness gap and improving overall performance in noisy conditions.

Abstract: Event detection (ED) systems are typically evaluated on clean, curated text, leaving their robustness to real-world noise largely unexplored, particularly for low-resource languages such as Bangla. We introduce a generalized Bangla news event ontology and a benchmark comprising 9,979 annotated sentences across 40 event subtypes, spanning clean news text, real-world Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) transcripts, and orthographically corrupted text. We systematically evaluate fine-tuned encoder-only models (BanglaBERT and XLM-R) alongside instruction-tuned decoder-only large language models (Llama 3 and Gemma 3). Our results reveal a clear architectural trade-off: encoder models achieve higher performance on clean text but degrade substantially under noise, whereas decoder-only LLMs are markedly more robust, particularly when event triggers are corrupted. We further show that embedding annotation guidelines during instruction tuning establishes a higher performance baseline on noisy text but yields inconsistent reductions in performance degradation across noisy conditions. Finally, model scaling consistently improves the robustness of decoder-only LLMs, while combined training on clean and noisy data serves as an effective regularization strategy that disproportionately benefits encoder architectures, significantly narrowing the robustness gap.

</details>


### [91] [Bridging Scientific Heritage: An Arabic--Russian Parallel Corpus and LLM Benchmark for Sustainable Knowledge Transfer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30943)
*M. K. Arabov*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces a benchmark for Arabic-Russian scientific translation and fine-tunes multilingual language models, achieving improved translation scores.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address the language barriers between Arabic and Russian scientific communities, which hinder research exchange and international collaboration, particularly in sustainability-related domains.

Method: The authors develop a hybrid parallel corpus of 27,000 sentence pairs and fine-tune multilingual models (mT5-base, NLLB-200, Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct) using LoRA with various ranks. They also evaluate few-shot prompting and release all resources for community use.

Result: The fine-tuned Qwen2.5-7B model with QLoRA (rank 8) achieves significant improvements in BLEU, chrF, BERTScore, and COMET metrics, surpassing the zero-shot baseline.

Conclusion: The proposed benchmark and models facilitate Arabic and Russian scientific communication, promoting cross-linguistic knowledge sharing and contributing to sustainable development goals (SDG 9 and 17).

Abstract: Russian and Arabic are among the major languages of scientific communication. Language barriers impede the exchange of research results between these communities, which affects international collaboration and the progress of sustainability-related research. We present a benchmark for Arabic--Russian scientific translation. The benchmark includes a hybrid parallel corpus of about 27,000 sentence pairs, compiled from scientific abstracts and general-domain texts (religion, news, conversations). We fine-tune three multilingual language models -- mT5-base (580M parameters), NLLB-200-distilled-1.3B (1.3B), and Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct (7B) -- using LoRA with ranks 8, 16, 32, and 64. The Qwen2.5-7B model with QLoRA (rank 8) yields BLEU 23.15, chrF 43.89, BERTScore 0.906, and COMET 0.758. These are +4.36 BLEU and +0.051 COMET above the zero-shot baseline. Few-shot prompting with three examples does not improve performance, indicating that domain-specific fine-tuning is required. We release the models, the corpus, and the evaluation code. By lowering the language barrier for scientific texts, the work enables knowledge exchange between Arabic-speaking and Russian-speaking researchers. It contributes to sustainable partnerships (UN SDG 17) and innovation infrastructure (SDG 9), aligning with the conference's focus on technology-driven sustainable development.

</details>


### [92] [Linguistic Distancing on Social Media: Indicators of Emotion Regulation Across Age Groups](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30957)
*Daniela Teodorescu,Saif M. Mohammad,Alona Fyshe*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies linguistic distancing as a marker of emotion regulation across age groups using social media text.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how emotion regulation varies with age by exploring linguistic distancing in text data and its implications for well-being and health interventions.

Method: Analyzed large-scale social media data to systematically study age-based variations in linguistic distancing markers as a proxy for emotion regulation.

Result: Linguistic distancing appears to increase with age, aligning with psychological findings of improved well-being in older individuals.

Conclusion: The study highlights the value of linguistic distancing as a marker of well-being and provides a foundation for health interventions through analysis of text data.

Abstract: Managing our emotional responses to events is key to emotional well-being, a process referred to as emotion regulation in psychology. Previous work has established that the degree to which we distance events is a type of emotion regulation. When we psychologically distance from events there can be markers in our language. These markers have been referred to as linguistic distancing. We build upon a previous metric to operationalize linguistic distancing, and explore how it changes across the lifespan. We explore this systematically by analyzing large amounts of social media text, a venue where people express their emotions. By investigating how distancing varies across age groups we can better understand how emotion regulation varies with age and provide initial benchmarks on social media data. We provide additional evidence further strengthening the hypothesis that linguistic distancing occurs in proportionally more instances with age. These findings align with past work in psychology which indicate improved well-being with older age. Better understanding how linguistic distancing changes with age is important because it functions as a marker of well-being and can inform effective health interventions. We provide a foundation for further exploring emotion regulation through linguistic distancing in text data.

</details>


### [93] [From Propositional to Perceptual Asymmetry: Extending Frictive Policy Optimization to Asymmetric Partial Information Dialogue](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30973)
*Yifan Zhu,Kyeongmin Rim,James Pustejovsky*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Frictive Policy Optimization (FPO) is extended to situations where dialogue participants have asymmetric information, revealing that perceptual perspectives are more critical for resolution of misunderstandings than omniscient viewpoints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper explores friction, such as misalignment and repairs, in collaborative dialogue as signals for building common ground rather than as noise. It aims to address dialogue scenarios with perceptual asymmetry, where participants interpret expressions differently due to partial information.

Method: The study extends FPO to perceptual asymmetry, analyzes dialogue tasks like the HCRC MapTask, and probes a large language model's (LLM) performance in resolving referential failure under asymmetric information contexts.

Result: FPO's friction-based approach works best within each participant's perspective, demonstrating distinct failure modes based on their landmark configurations. The informed, single viewpoint outperforms omniscient perspectives in handling misunderstandings.

Conclusion: The findings suggest leveraging individual information perspectives in dialogue optimization, proposing refined annotation techniques to better classify pending states and capture alignment in perceptual asymmetry.

Abstract: Frictive Policy Optimization (FPO; Pustejovsky et al., 2025) treats friction in collaborative dialogue -- misalignment, misunderstanding, repair -- as an epistemic signal essential to common-ground construction, rather than noise to be minimized. However, FPO and its implementations assume shared perceptual contexts, where friction arises from differently interpreted propositions over the same scene, which we define as propositional asymmetry. We extend FPO to perceptual asymmetry, where participants hold asymmetric partial information and the same referring expression yields different denotations depending on whose information state grounds the reference. We evaluate this through cross-corpora analysis and LLM probing on referentially asymmetric dialogue tasks, primarily the HCRC MapTask (Anderson et al., 1991). We find that FPO's friction functional is empirically valid only when evaluated from within each participant's information horizon: different landmark configurations produce qualitatively distinct grounding failure modes, with a small class of ambiguous configurations driving a disproportionate share of misunderstandings through trajectories that appear successful but silently diverge. The LLM probe confirms that having the "right perspective" matters more than having all perspectives: the informed single viewpoint outperforms omniscient access to both participants' contexts. We propose two annotation refinements: subtype decomposition of pending grounding states and accommodation-aware alignment classification.

</details>


### [94] [Measuring Judgment Quality in Natural-Language Explanations: Evidence from Forecasting Tournaments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30987)
*Christopher W. Karvetski,Sheldon S. Huang,Simas Kučinskas,Nadja Flechner,Jingyu Hu,Philip Tetlock,Ezra Karger*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces Explanation Quality Markers (EQMs) to measure the quality of expert explanations in forecasting tournaments using large language models, showing EQMs predict forecasting accuracy better than traditional methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The need to measure the quality of written expert judgments effectively in decision-making processes, especially in forecasting environments, where traditional methods struggle.

Method: The authors developed EQMs—a set of 60 reasoning patterns scored by large language models—and tested them using over 55,000 forecast-rationale pairs from a forecasting tournament, comparing LLM scoring to traditional text-analysis methods.

Result: EQMs consistently outperformed pre-LLM methods, predicting accuracy at the forecast and forecaster levels, and were the best indicator of quality at the forecast level. Around 90% of pattern-accuracy correlations aligned with initial hypotheses, and results transferred to another study.

Conclusion: EQMs offer a scalable and interpretable tool for extracting quality indicators from written rationales, outperforming many traditional methods and providing a strong predictor of forecasting accuracy.

Abstract: Decision-makers routinely rely on expert judgments accompanied by written explanations, yet explanation quality is difficult to measure at scale. Forecasting tournaments offer a natural testing ground: probabilistic judgments are paired with natural-language rationales and scored against realized outcomes. We introduce Explanation Quality Markers (EQMs), a set of sixty theory-guided reasoning patterns scored by large language models (LLMs). In a pre-registered analysis of over 55,000 forecast-rationale pairs from a multiyear forecasting tournament, EQMs predict accuracy at both the forecast and forecaster levels, consistently outperforming pre-LLM text-analysis methods. More than 90% of statistically significant pattern-level EQM-accuracy correlations match our directional hypotheses. The signal is asymmetric: EQMs identify likely underperformers more reliably than they distinguish the very best forecasters. Benchmarked against traditional indicators of forecasting skill, EQMs are the strongest predictor at the forecast level and competitive at the forecaster level, though weaker than prior accuracy. Human ratings of rationale quality are less consistently correlated with accuracy and place disproportionate weight on rationale length. Results transfer to an independent forecasting study. EQMs provide a scalable, interpretable method for extracting judgment-relevant information from written explanations.

</details>


### [95] [Wait, am I Being Fair? Characterizing Deductive Stereotyping and Mitigating It with Fair-GCG](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30989)
*Naihao Deng,Yilun Zhu,Joan Nwatu,Clayton Scott,Rada Mihalcea*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper discusses a fairness issue in large language models (LLMs) called 'deductive stereotyping' and proposes a reasoning-time injection framework, Fair-GCG, to mitigate this bias.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite improvements in fairness, LLMs still display biases due to their application of population-level stereotypes to individual cases. This paper aims to address this specific failure mode and improve fairness in reasoning.

Method: The authors introduce a reasoning-time injection framework, supported by a system called Fair-GCG, which identifies effective phrases to guide the models toward fairness-aware reasoning.

Result: Fair-GCG enhances reasoning fairness, reduces bias, improves performance on fairness benchmarks, and demonstrates adaptability to both open-ended generation and real-world fairness tasks.

Conclusion: The proposed method successfully steers LLMs toward more fairness-conscious reasoning, showcasing utility across tasks and model sizes.

Abstract: Warning: This paper contains several toxic and offensive statements. While reasoning generally improves fairness in recent large language models (LLMs), failures persist. In this work, we identify a failure mode, deductive stereotyping, in which models apply population-level statistical regularities to individual cases, producing logically coherent yet socially biased inferences. We provide a statistical interpretation of this phenomenon. To steer models toward fairness-aware reasoning, we propose a reasoning-time injection framework. We further introduce Fair-GCG to systematically discover effective injection phrases. Injection phrases discovered by Fair-GCG improve performance across multiple fairness benchmarks, generalize from smaller to larger LLMs, improves reasoning-level fairness, reduces bias in open-ended generation, and transfer to real-world fairness-sensitive tasks.

</details>


### [96] [CORTEX: Token-Level Hallucination Detection in RAG via Comparative Internal Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31033)
*Kazuaki Furumai,Shuichiro Haruta,Kazunori Matsumoto,Daisuke Kamisaka*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: CORTEX is a method for fine-grained hallucination detection in RAG outputs by analyzing token-level grounding using internal LLM representations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hallucinations in RAG outputs often occur in specific localized spans rather than the entire text. A need exists for precise detection methods to identify and localize these instances accurately to improve reliability.

Method: CORTEX analyzes token sensitivities to retrieved documents by comparing LLM representations with and without access to these documents. It propagates context-derived information from preceding tokens and includes a smoothing mechanism to enhance span-consistent detection.

Result: CORTEX significantly improves token-level hallucination detection in experiments across two RAG benchmarks and three LLMs, with all components driving consistent performance gains.

Conclusion: CORTEX enhances the precision of hallucination detection in RAG tasks by incorporating token-level grounding, context propagation, and smoothing, making outputs more reliable and consistent.

Abstract: In this paper, we propose CORTEX, a token-level hallucination detection method for Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). In long-form RAG outputs, hallucinations often arise in localized spans rather than throughout an entire response. CORTEX therefore identifies ungrounded content at the token level, enabling fine-grained localization of hallucinations. The key intuition behind CORTEX is that tokens grounded in retrieved documents should be more strongly influenced by those documents than hallucinated tokens. To capture this document-induced effect, CORTEX compares internal representations of a large language model (LLM) under two conditions: with and without the retrieved documents. Instead of relying solely on each token's immediate sensitivity to the retrieved documents, CORTEX also leverages the propagation of document-grounded information through preceding tokens, reducing false positives for tokens whose evidence has already been absorbed into the context. Finally, CORTEX applies post-processing smoothing step that models the tendency of hallucination labels to persist over contiguous spans, reducing local noise and encouraging span-consistent predictions. Experiments on two RAG benchmarks and three LLMs show that CORTEX substantially improves token-level hallucination detection, with each component consistently contributing to performance gains.

</details>


### [97] [Truth or Sophistry? LoFa: A Benchmark for LLM Robustness Against Logical Fallacies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31039)
*Xudong Shen,Li Yuan,Ye Chen,Xin Wu,Yi Cai,Zhiyong Wu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Large Language Models (LLMs) are tested for their resistance to logical fallacies using a new benchmark called LoFa.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper investigates the resilience of LLMs against manipulative linguistic patterns like logical fallacies, which has been underexplored compared to their semantic capabilities.

Method: Introduced LoFa, a benchmark created via a multi-agent pipeline, and Logical Fallacy Resistance at k (LFR@k), a metric for quantifying robustness. Fallacies are tested through factual questions and adversarial debate frameworks.

Result: LLMs show differing robustness to various fallacy types, revealing distinct vulnerability profiles.

Conclusion: LLMs are variably susceptible to logical fallacies, underlining the need for evaluating and improving their robustness against manipulative arguments.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) exhibit strong semantic capabilities, yet their resilience to manipulative linguistic patterns such as logical fallacies remains underexplored. Prior work has primarily examined whether LLMs can identify or classify fallacies, leaving their robustness against fallacious persuasion insufficiently studied. To address this gap, we introduce LoFa (Logical Fallacy), a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating LLM robustness against fallacies. LoFa is constructed through a multi-agent pipeline that pairs factual questions with fallacious arguments, and is accompanied by a multi-round debate framework for assessing model resilience under sustained adversarial persuasion. To disentangle fallacy robustness from a model's inherent knowledge limitations, we further propose Logical Fallacy Resistance at k (LFR@k), a metric that quantifies resistance to fallacious attacks. Experiments show that LLMs exhibit varying levels of robustness across different fallacy types, revealing distinct vulnerability profiles among models.

</details>


### [98] [A Semantic-Layer-Mediated Agent for Natural Language to SQL over Heterogeneous Enterprise Databases](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31041)
*Ha Jeong Kim,Saksonita Khoeurn,Ye Ji Yoon*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces a semantic-layer-based approach to Natural Language-to-SQL (NL2SQL) for complex enterprise databases, using a simplified intermediate representation called Semantic Model Query (SMQ) and achieving high execution accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the challenges posed by real-world enterprise databases, which are far more complex than academic benchmarks due to factors like large schema sizes, cryptic column names, and diverse SQL dialects.

Method: The authors developed an NL2SQL system that uses a semantic layer with a compact intermediate representation (SMQ). This decouples semantic intent from physical SQL generation and enables compatibility with various SQL backends.

Result: The system achieved 94.15% execution accuracy on the Spider2-snow benchmark and ranked third on the official leaderboard, showing significant improvement over schema-only NL2SQL approaches.

Conclusion: Leveraging a semantic layer with SMQ enhances query grounding while mitigating challenges in dealing with complex enterprise database schemas. This approach balances improved accuracy with risks of overfitting.

Abstract: Natural language-to-SQL (NL2SQL) over real-world enterprise databases remains significantly more challenging than on academic benchmarks. Enterprise schemas often contain hundreds of physical tables with cryptic column names, heterogeneous SQL dialects, and complex analytical workloads requiring nested aggregations, temporal reasoning, and multi-table joins. We present a semantic-layer-mediated NL2SQL agent that decouples semantic intent from physical SQL execution. Rather than generating SQL directly over raw schemas, the agent reasons over a curated semantic layer through a compact intermediate representation called the Semantic Model Query (SMQ). A deterministic compiler translates each SMQ into dialect-specific SQL, providing verified building blocks that the agent composes into the final query. The system employs a constrained think-act loop, supports SQLite, BigQuery, and Snowflake backends, and is integrated into an end-to-end evaluation framework. Using Gemini 3 Pro, the system achieves 94.15% execution accuracy on the 547-task Spider2-snow benchmark, ranking third on the official leaderboard and substantially outperforming schema-only approaches. We describe the system architecture, SMQ representation, agent workflow, evaluation results, and discuss semantic-layer quality and the trade-off between improved grounding and overfitting.

</details>


### [99] [Reference-Based Prosody and Rhythm Evaluation for Spoken Dialogue Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31055)
*Ashish Hallur,Thomas Thebaud,Georgi Tinchev,Venkatesh Ravichandran,Laureano Moro-Velazquez*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper addresses the challenge of evaluating speech-to-speech AI agents by creating interpretable measures for conversational prosody and rhythm using matched human reference data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve evaluation methods for S2S AI agents, which lack speech-native measures for conversational traits like prosody and rhythm, and are poorly calibrated with human data.

Method: The researchers used metrics from over 4000 hours of English conversation to define percentile-based evaluations of S2S outputs, comparing them against matched human reference regimes.

Result: Matched references yield more accurate evaluations of conversational dynamics compared to pooled references by minimizing false flags and improving interpretability.

Conclusion: The proposed evaluation protocol enhances assessment of S2S AI in terms of behavioral plausibility while supporting perceptual and user-centered evaluations.

Abstract: Speech-to-speech (S2S) AI agents are advancing rapidly, yet evaluation lacks interpretable speech-native measures for conversational prosody and rhythm. Because $F_0$, speaking rate, articulation rate, and pausing shift with model-predicted speaker traits and interaction state, pooled human statistics can be poorly calibrated for evaluating a particular output. Using 4000+ hours of dyadic English conversation from the Seamless Interaction dataset, we construct matched reference regimes for $F_0$ mean, $F_0$ expressivity, speech rate, articulation rate, pause ratio, and mean pause duration. We then define a percentile-based evaluation protocol: extract the same metrics from an S2S output waveform, compare them to the closest matched human reference stratum, and report percentile deviations or 5th-95th percentile out-of-regime flags. On held-out human rows, pooled references over-flag state-conditioned $F_0$ expressivity and rhythm, while matched references return flag rates closer to the nominal 10% and make deviation direction interpretable. These outputs serve as behavioral plausibility checks that complement, rather than replace, perceptual and user-centered evaluation.

</details>


### [100] [Exploring the relationship between team institutional composition and novelty in academic papers based on fine-grained knowledge entities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31058)
*Ziling Chen,Chengzhi Zhang,Heng Zhang,Yi Zhao,Chen Yang,Yang Yang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper examines how the composition of author teams, particularly in terms of institutional affiliation, affects the fine-grained novelty of academic papers in natural language processing (NLP). It finds that mixed academic-industrial teams produce more novel papers than solely industrial ones and provides insights into the specific knowledge entity types associated with novelty.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to understand the role of institutional composition within author teams in enhancing the novelty of academic papers, addressing a research gap on how teams' affiliations influence innovation at a granular level.

Method: The authors classify teams into academic, industrial, and mixed academic-industrial categories, extract four fine-grained knowledge entities (methods, datasets, tools, and metrics) from NLP papers, and analyze novelty based on combinations of these entities.

Result: Collaboration between academia and industry yields more novel papers compared to purely industrial teams. Academic-industrial teams focus on method-metric combinations, while industrial teams emphasize method-tool combinations.

Conclusion: The study highlights the value of mixed academic-industrial collaboration in fostering paper novelty and provides actionable insights for enhancing research quality through strategic team composition.

Abstract: The composition of author teams is an important factor influencing the novelty of academic papers. However, existing studies have paid limited attention to the role of institutional composition, and most novelty measures remain at a general level, making it difficult to explain the specific sources and types of novelty in papers. Taking the field of natural language processing as an example, this study investigates the relationship between team institutional composition and the fine-grained novelty of academic papers. Author teams are classified into three types: academic institutions, industrial institutions, and mixed academic and industrial institutions. Four types of fine-grained knowledge entities are extracted from full-text papers, including methods, datasets, tools, and metrics. The novelty of papers is then measured based on entity combinations, and pairwise combinations of different entity types are further analyzed to examine their contributions to novel papers. The results show that, in the field of natural language processing, collaboration between industrial and academic institutions is more likely to produce novel papers than purely industrial collaboration. From the perspective of fine-grained knowledge entities, mixed academic and industrial teams pay more attention to the novelty of method-metric combinations, whereas industrial teams pay more attention to the novelty of method-tool combinations. This study reveals the relationship between institutional team composition and paper novelty through fine-grained novelty measurement, providing useful evidence for improving paper quality and promoting industry-academia-research collaboration.

</details>


### [101] [Building a Multimodal Dataset of Academic Paper for Keyword Extraction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31069)
*Jingyu Zhang,Xinyi Yan,Yi Xiang,Yingyi Zhang,Chengzhi Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Traditional keyword extraction focuses solely on text, omitting visual and audio features, which limits accuracy and learning capabilities. This study introduces a 1000-sample multimodal dataset containing text, images, audio, and keywords for academic papers. Multimodal integration significantly enhances extraction performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Keyword extraction often neglects visual and audio data, limiting models and hindering the development of multimodal approaches due to a scarcity of datasets.

Method: The researchers constructed a novel multimodal academic paper dataset (1000 samples) containing text, images, audio, and keywords. They applied unsupervised and supervised keyword extraction methods across modalities and fused multimodal information.

Result: Experimental results validate that fusing text from texts, images, and audio improves keyword extraction performance and highlights unique characteristics across modalities.

Conclusion: Integrating multimodal information significantly boosts keyword extraction effectiveness, encouraging further advancements in multimodal approaches.

Abstract: Up to this point, keyword extraction task typically relies solely on textual data. Neglecting visual details and audio features from image and audio modalities leads to deficiencies in information richness and overlooks potential correlations, thereby constraining the model's ability to learn representations of the data and the accuracy of model predictions. Furthermore, the currently available multimodal datasets for keyword extraction task are particularly scarce, further hindering the progress of research on multimodal keyword extraction task. Therefore, this study constructs a multimodal dataset of academic paper consisting of 1000 samples, with each sample containing paper text, images, audios and keywords. Based on unsupervised and supervised methods of keyword extraction, experiments are conducted using textual data from papers, as well as text extracted from images and audio. The aim is to investigate the differences in performance in keyword extraction task with respect to different modal information and the fusion of multimodal information. The experimental results indicate that text from different modalities exhibits distinct characteristics in the model. The concatenation of paper text, image text and audio text can effectively enhance the keyword extraction performance of academic papers.

</details>


### [102] [Triospect: A Three-Dimensional Framework for Robust Statistical AI-Generated Text Detection Against Diverse Attacks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31074)
*Guangsheng Bao,Lihua Rong,Yanbin Zhao,Xiao Yu,Qiji Zhou,Yue Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents Triospect, a new approach for detecting AI-generated text, which is more robust against diverse attack strategies than existing detectors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AI-generated text detectors are susceptible to adversarial attacks that modify textual characteristics, making robust detection highly needed.

Method: The Triospect Detection Framework leverages three perspectives—content (core ideas), expression (stylistic elements), and standard textual analysis—to improve detection robustness.

Result: Triospect outperforms a strong baseline detector, showing significant improvement of up to 22.3% in AUROC and 22% in TPR01 across various benchmarks and attack scenarios.

Conclusion: This study introduces a novel statistical detection approach that significantly enhances the reliability of AI-generated text detection against adversarial manipulations.

Abstract: Existing AI-generated text detectors are vulnerable to attacks that manipulate textual characteristics. In this study, we propose a novel Triospect Detection Framework by using additional perspectives of content (core ideas) and expression (stylistic elements) within a given text. Experiments on two benchmarks involving 17 attacks, 12 domains, and 17 source models demonstrate that Triospect is robust against these attacks. It improves the strong baseline by a significant margin of 22.3% (AUROC) and 13% (TPR01) on the Humanize-16K after-attack subset, and by 9.1% (AUROC) and 22% (TPR01) on the adversarial RAID. This framework marks a pioneering effort in statistical methods to enhance detection reliability against attacks. We release our data and code at https://github.com/baoguangsheng/triospect.

</details>


### [103] [When Reranking Hurts: Uncertainty-Based Gating for Few-Shot Reranking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31087)
*Orian Dabod,Amir Cohen,Gabriel Stanovsky*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces a novel approach to few-shot selection, demonstrating that reranking examples can sometimes degrade performance. Instead, a training-free gated method is proposed to optimize reranking based on model uncertainty.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the issue of performance degradation caused by reranking few-shot examples and explore how computational costs can be reduced while maintaining or improving performance.

Method: Introduce a Training-Free Gated Reranking approach that utilizes model uncertainty to decide when reranking few-shot examples should be applied.

Result: The proposed method reduces computational costs by 15%-80% and improves average performance by up to 2% across 8 LLMs, 7 NLU datasets, and 9 MT domain-language combinations.

Conclusion: Higher computational cost does not necessarily guarantee better performance, and reranking should be selectively applied, focusing on high-uncertainty instances for optimal results.

Abstract: Few-shot selection typically assumes that reranking retrieved examples always improves performance. We challenge this view by identifying that the expensive reranking step can in fact degrade performance. Instead, we propose \emph{Training-Free Gated Reranking}, which decides whether to rerank the few-shot examples based on the model's uncertainty. Extensive experiments across 8 LLMs, covering 7 NLU datasets and 9 MT domain-language combinations, demonstrate that our approach reduces computational costs by 15\%-80\% while improving average performance by up to 2\%. These findings indicate that higher computational cost does not guarantee better performance, and that reranking is most beneficial when targeted at high-uncertainty instances.

</details>


### [104] [What Counts as an Error? Dual-Reference Benchmarking for Atypical ASR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31112)
*Hawau Olamide Toyin,Srinivasan Umesh,Hanan Aldarmaki*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper addresses the dual transcription references (verbatim and intended) in ASR evaluation for atypical speech and benchmarks 11 models to highlight the differences in performance and ranking based on these references.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate how the dual transcription references (verbatim and intended) affect ASR evaluations and rankings, especially for atypical stuttered speech, and emphasize the importance of selecting appropriate references suited to use-cases.

Method: Benchmarking 11 ASR models (from encoder-decoder, CTC, and transducer families) on atypical stuttered speech using both verbatim and intended transcription references.

Result: Found significant disparities in ASR model performance and rankings based on whether verbatim or intended transcription styles were used for evaluation.

Conclusion: The choice of transcription reference (verbatim vs. intended) significantly impacts model selection and ASR evaluation for atypical speech, making it critical to align reference choices with specific use-cases.

Abstract: ASR systems have been often reported to underperform on atypical speech. An often conflated compounding factor is the existence of two valid transcription references: verbatim (actual produced speech, including repetitions/prolongations) and intended (the canonical form of the text with disfluencies removed) in atypical speech recognition depending on context and use-case. Most ASR evaluations conflate this duality into a single ground truth and reward systems that delete disfluencies, ignoring verbatim faithfulness. We benchmark 11 ASR models from encoder-decoder, CTC and transducer families using both verbatim and intended references on atypical stuttered speech as a case study. Our quantitative assessment underlines the disparity in model performance and rankings using the two transcript styles. Through this analysis, we highlight the importance of selecting a suitable transcription reference for valid model selection depending on the use-case, particularly for atypical ASR.

</details>


### [105] [SeKV: Resolution-Adaptive KV Cache with Hierarchical Semantic Memory for Long-Context LLM Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31145)
*Amirhossein Abaskohi,Giuseppe Carenini,Peter West,Yuhang He*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces SeKV, a semantic KV cache designed for efficient and accurate compression of large language model memory without discarding details, enabling better performance and GPU memory reduction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation of this paper is to address the memory bottleneck caused by the KV cache in large language models when operating over long contexts, where existing compression solutions fail to balance efficiency and accurate preservation of token-level detail.

Method: The proposed SeKV method organizes contexts into entropy-guided semantic spans stored across a GPU-CPU hierarchy, using summary vectors for coarse routing and low-rank SVD for token-level reconstruction, along with a trained zoom-in mechanism for query-relevant expansion.

Result: SeKV outperforms the strongest existing semantic compression baseline by 5.9% across four benchmarks and reduces GPU memory usage by 53.3% compared to full KV caching at a sequence length of 128K.

Conclusion: SeKV offers a resolution-adaptive semantic KV cache that achieves efficient GPU memory usage and maintains precise token-level reconstruction, with minimal additional trainable parameters, making it a scalable solution for long-context large language models.

Abstract: Large language models increasingly operate over long contexts, where the KV cache becomes a dominant memory bottleneck: its size grows linearly with sequence length and must be retained throughout decoding, making full GPU caching prohibitively expensive without compression. Existing KV cache compression methods struggle to balance efficiency with faithful context preservation. Token eviction discards information, while semantic grouping fixes compression decisions at prefill time; neither can recover token-level detail from a compressed span once it becomes relevant during generation. As a solution, we propose SeKV, a resolution-adaptive semantic KV cache that organizes context into entropy-guided semantic spans and stores them across a GPU-CPU memory hierarchy without discarding information. Each span keeps a lightweight summary vector on GPU for coarse routing and a low-rank SVD basis on CPU for on-demand token-level reconstruction. A trained zoom-in mechanism selectively expands query-relevant spans during decoding, enabling precise retrieval without materializing the full KV cache on GPU. SeKV enables adaptive token-level reconstruction while keeping the base LLM fully frozen and adding fewer than 0.05% trainable parameters. Across four benchmarks, SeKV improves over the strongest semantic compression baseline by 5.9% on average while reducing GPU memory by 53.3% versus full KV caching at 128K context. Code is available on https://github.com/AmirAbaskohi/SeKV.

</details>


### [106] [TAG-DLM: Diffusion Language Models for Text-Attributed Graph Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31166)
*Lingjie Chen,Yuanchen Bei,Haobo Xu,Yanjun Zhao,Yuzhong Chen,Hanghang Tong*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper presents a novel method that integrates text and graph reasoning in a unified model, surpassing existing approaches on key benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for text-attributed graphs struggle to efficiently combine textual and structural reasoning, often treating them as separate components.

Method: The proposed method uses a masked diffusion language model that incorporates graph structure directly into textual reasoning through a topology attention mask, enabling seamless support for multiple tasks without fine-tuning.

Result: The method consistently outperforms baseline models, including graph neural networks and LLM-based approaches, across multiple TAG benchmarks and tasks, achieving up to a 3.9-point improvement.

Conclusion: This unified approach effectively bridges text and graph reasoning, providing a versatile and superior solution to handling text-attributed graphs.

Abstract: Text-attributed graphs (TAGs), where each node carries a natural language description, require models to jointly reason over text and graph topology. Existing approaches often handle the two modalities separately: graph neural networks operate on shallow text features, while hybrids of LLMs and graphs use the language model mainly as a text encoder and delegate structure learning to a separate graph module. We propose method that unifies textual reasoning and graph message passing within a masked diffusion language model, a language model with bidirectional attention and generative decoding. For each graph instance, method linearises a sampled local neighbourhood into a token sequence and injects graph structure through a topology attention mask, which realises message passing over the graph. Because the diffusion language model can both interpret and generate text, the method adapts to different tasks simply by changing the prompt, supporting node classification, link prediction, and cross-dataset transfer with no target-specific fine-tuning.
  Experiments show that method outperforms graph neural networks, graph transformers, and LLM-based baselines on all three TAG benchmarks across two tasks, improving over the strongest baseline by up to 3.9 points.

</details>


### [107] [Gated Multi-Graph Fusion via Graph Attention Networks for Alzheimer's Disease Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31186)
*Jinyu Li,Xiao Wei,Bin Wen,Kai Li,Yuqin Lin,Xiaobao Wang,Longbiao Wang,Jianwu Dang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper presents a Multi-View Gated Graph Attention Network for Alzheimer's diagnosis, achieving 90% accuracy using spontaneous speech analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenges in detecting Alzheimer's through non-invasive speech analysis, particularly focusing on non-linear structural disruptions and clinical heterogeneity in pathological language.

Method: The model constructs semantic, dependency, and co-occurrence graphs from audio transcriptions using ASR, quantified by Pointwise Mutual Information. An adaptive gating mechanism integrates these views dynamically.

Result: The proposed system is tested on the ADReSSo dataset, achieving a classification accuracy of 90% and highlighting the importance of the PMI-based graph and adaptive gating.

Conclusion: This method effectively captures nuanced speech disorders in Alzheimer's patients and demonstrates robust performance across clinical diversity, offering a promising diagnostic tool.

Abstract: Spontaneous speech is a vital non-invasive biomarker for Alzheimer's Disease (AD), yet many systems overlook non-linear structural disruptions and clinical heterogeneity in pathological language. We propose a Multi-View Gated Graph Attention Network that transcribes audio via Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) to construct semantic, dependency, and co-occurrence graphs, characterizing speech through a "content-structure-flow" framework. Notably, the co-occurrence graph leverages Pointwise Mutual Information (PMI) from a normative corpus to quantify narrative logic and linguistic deviation. To address symptomatic diversity, an adaptive gated fusion mechanism dynamically integrates these views. Evaluated on the ADReSSo dataset, our model achieves 90.00% accuracy. Ablation results confirm that the PMI-based graph and heterogeneity-aware gating are essential for robust classification across diverse clinical populations. Our source code is publicly available at https://github.com/opeacc/AD.

</details>


### [108] [Can LLMs Imagine Moral Alternatives Beyond Binary Dilemmas?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31213)
*Jongchan Choi,Nari Yang,Sung Soo Park,Jaemin Cho,Han Seoyoung,Haerin Shin,Jun-Hyung Park*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper investigates how large language models (LLMs) navigate moral dilemmas and introduces MoralAltDataset, which provides alternative ways of resolving dilemmas. It finds that LLMs and humans prefer compromise options and that LLM-generated alternatives can rival human-authored ones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the need for LLMs to handle moral dilemmas better by not only choosing between given options but also generating creative and ethical alternatives.

Method: The authors created MoralAltDataset containing 307 dilemmas with compromise and reframed alternatives. They compared human and LLM judgments, and evaluated the quality of LLM-generated alternatives against human-authored ones using pairwise preference and expert criteria.

Result: LLMs and humans tend to prefer compromise options over the original dilemmas. LLM-generated alternatives often surpass human-authored ones, meeting ethical and structural criteria, while revealing trade-offs in feasibility.

Conclusion: Introducing alternative options enriches moral deliberation for LLMs. These models can effectively propose ethical compromises, though trade-offs exist in practicality and structural quality.

Abstract: As large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as moral advisors and agents, they need to address dilemmas between two competing values. However, existing research on LLMs with moral dilemmas overlooks a central aspect of human moral cognition: the ability to imagine alternatives that move beyond the given options. We introduce MoralAltDataset, a dataset of 307 moral dilemmas spanning narrative Advisor dilemmas and AI-facing Agent dilemmas, each augmented with compromise and reframed alternatives. We first examine whether humans and LLMs shift their judgments when such alternatives are introduced. Across 15 LLMs, we find that compromise alternatives are often preferred over either original option, substantially reshaping moral choice. We then evaluate the quality of LLM-generated alternatives against human-authored ones using pairwise preference and expert-based criteria. Results show that LLM-generated alternatives are often preferred and better satisfy fine-grained structural and ethical criteria, while revealing trade-offs between structural quality and practical feasibility.

</details>


### [109] [Probing Stylistic Appropriation using Large Language Models: An Evaluation Framework for Copyright Infringement under EU Law](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31250)
*Noah Scharrenberg,Chang Sun*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper bridges the gap between generative AI safeguards and EU copyright doctrine by introducing PSALM, an LLM-as-a-judge framework that evaluates broader copyright risks beyond verbatim memorization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the mismatch between current safeguards focusing on verbatim copying and the broader EU copyright doctrine that includes stylistic and narrative elements, which leaves compliance gaps for large language models (LLMs).

Method: They introduced PSALM, a framework with ten evaluators analyzing computational overlap, stylistic, and content dimensions, as well as statutory exceptions, to operationalize EU copyright standards.

Result: PSALM applied to Llama~3.2 models showed that instruction-tuned LLMs possess baseline stylistic similarity, fine-tuning induces systematic stylistic appropriation, and unlearning methods reduce but do not eliminate residual patterns.

Conclusion: Current safeguards targeting literal copying are insufficient; tools like PSALM offer a legally informed framework for evaluating copyright risks, but legal expert validation remains crucial for linking automated metrics to infringement determinations.

Abstract: Large language models (LLM) trained on web-scale corpora generate output that may infringe copyright, yet existing technical safeguards focus narrowly on verbatim memorisation. EU copyright doctrine applies a broader standards: substantial similarity, which extends to stylistic choices, narrative structure, and creative elaboration. This mismatch between what current methods detect and what the law protects leaves a significant compliance gap. We introduce PSALM, an LLM-as-a-judge framework that operationalises EU copyright doctrine through ten evaluators assessing computational overlap, stylistic dimensions (writing style, narrative voice), content dimensions (character, plot, scene, world building), and statutory exceptions (parody, pastiche, quotation, scènes à faire). Applying PSALM to Llama~3.2 models fine-tuned on translated historical Dutch literary works, we find that: 1) instruction-tuned models exhibit non-trivial baseline stylistic similarity prior to corpus exposure; 2) fine-tuning induces systematic stylistic appropriation across all infringement-relevant dimensions, extending beyond verbatim memorisation to abstract narrative patterns; 3) Negative Preference Optimisation unlearning substantially reduces similarity but leaves detectable residual stylistic patterns. These findings indicate that safeguards targeting literal copying alone are insufficient to mitigate broader copyright risks. PSALM provides infrastructure for auditable, legally informed compliance evaluation, though the relationship between automated similarity scores and infringement determinations requires validation by legal experts. This work bridges qualitative legal standards and quantitative technical measurement, exposing fundamental tensions between generative AI and EU intellectual property law.

</details>


### [110] [When the Database Fails: Prompting LLM Dialogue Agents for Safe Recovery in Task-Oriented Dialogue](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31307)
*Mohammad Alijanpour Shalmani,Alale Rezvani Boroujeni,Jiann Shiun Yuan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper addresses the issue of unsafe and hallucinated responses by large language models in task-oriented dialogue when database calls fail, proposing a prompting-based recovery strategy that reduces these issues by 50% on certain benchmarks without requiring retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to tackle the unsafe and unrealistic outputs of language models when database operations fail or return mismatched/wrong results in task-oriented dialogues, aiming for safer and more grounded responses.

Method: The study uses a lightweight prompting-based recovery approach applied to six open-weight model families under four database failure conditions. It involves guided recovery prompts informed by structured database statuses and evaluates them using fault-injected benchmarks on two datasets (MultiWOZ 2.2 and SGD).

Result: The proposed Guided-Retry strategy reduces hallucination rates by 50% on MultiWOZ and 42% on SGD without retraining, but challenges remain with residual hallucination (6-37%) and wrong-domain failures.

Conclusion: The prompting-based technique improves robustness to database inconsistencies across datasets and models but does not fully eliminate hallucination, highlighting the need for further research.

Abstract: Large language models used in task-oriented dialogue often produce fluent but unsafe responses when backend database calls fail, return empty results, or surface mismatched information, inventing venues, confirmations, or booking details not grounded in the database. We study a lightweight prompting-based recovery approach that improves robustness without retraining or additional model calls. We compare three response strategies, including a guided recovery prompt conditioned on structured database status, across six open-weight model families (DeepSeek-R1, Gemma-2, Llama-3, Mistral, Phi-3, and Qwen-2.5) and four database conditions: empty result, wrong-domain retrieval, API error, and clean retrieval. Using fault-injected benchmarks built on two structurally different datasets, MultiWOZ 2.2 (5 domains) and SGD (20 domains), we find that naive agents hallucinate on 30.5% of failure turns on MultiWOZ and 20.9% on SGD. Our Guided-Retry strategy reduces hallucination by 50% on MultiWOZ (30.5 to 15.3%) and by 42% on SGD (20.9 to 12.2%) without retraining. However, residual hallucination remains substantial (6-37% across models), with wrong-domain failures the hardest case. Results are consistent across both datasets and all six model families, and human annotation shows substantial agreement while supporting the validity of the automatic commitment-safety metric.

</details>


### [111] [LOPA: Enhancing Spoken Language Assessment via Latent Ordinal Prototype Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31310)
*Hong-Yun Lin,Fu-An Chao,Bi-Cheng Yan,Berlin Chen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces a framework combining Latent Ordinal Prototype Alignment (LOPA) and Semantic-Anchored Layer Routing (SALR) to enhance Spoken Language Assessment (SLA) without relying on large Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current SLA approaches based on MLLMs overlook the intrinsic ordinal structure of language acquisition, and scaling multimodal models is computationally intensive.

Method: The framework employs LOPA as a regularizer imposing ordinal priors on latent space and SALR for adaptively utilizing multi-depth representations from a frozen Whisper encoder, avoiding the need for fine-tuning massive models.

Result: The framework achieves an RMSE of 0.361, comparable to billion-parameter systems, with interpretable and criterion-aligned performance.

Conclusion: LOPA and SALR provide an efficient, ordinal-aware alternative to scaling-focused SLA models, offering interpretable results without requiring extensive computational resources.

Abstract: Fueled by increasing model scale and multimodal inputs, Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have emerged as a promising paradigm for Spoken Language Assessment (SLA). While effective, this paradigm often overlooks the intrinsic ordinal structure of language acquisition. This paper works around the necessity of large-scale MLLMs by introducing Latent Ordinal Prototype Alignment (LOPA) for SLA, a prototype-based regularizer that enforces an ordinal geometric prior directly on the latent space. Coupled with Semantic-Anchored Layer Routing (SALR), which adaptively harvests multi-depth representations from a frozen Whisper encoder, our framework achieves an RMSE of 0.361. This performance rivals billion-parameter systems without the need for LLM-based fine-tuning. Further analysis reveals that SALR's synergy with LOPA offers interpretable, criterion-aligned preferences, thereby supporting an efficient and ordinal-aware modeling alternative to current scaling-centric models for SLA.

</details>


### [112] [BlockPilot: Instance-Adaptive Policy Learning for Diffusion-based Speculative Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31315)
*Hao Zhang,Yiming Hu,Yong Wang,Mingqiao Mo,Xin Xiao,Xiangxiang Chu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces BlockPilot, a sample-adaptive policy for speculative decoding, optimizing block size selection to enhance performance and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve the performance of speculative decoding by addressing the limitation of fixed block sizes and uniform decoding strategies across inputs.

Method: BlockPilot predicts the optimal block size in speculative decoding using lightweight policy learning based on prefilling representations to adaptively improve performance per input.

Result: BlockPilot enhances speculative decoding by achieving a significant 4.20× speedup and an acceptance length of 5.92 on Qwen3-4B under temperature T=1.

Conclusion: The proposed method is efficient, requires minimal overhead, and is plug-and-play, offering consistent improvements in decoding performance and computational speed.

Abstract: Speculative decoding accelerates inference by using a lightweight draft model to generate candidate tokens in parallel, and are then verified by the target model, enabling lossless acceleration. Recently, diffusion-based speculative decoding further improves parallelism by generating multiple tokens per forward pass via block-level diffusion, achieving state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance. However, existing methods adopt a fixed inference block size and assume a uniform optimal decoding strategy across all inputs. In this paper, we show that this assumption is suboptimal, as the optimal block size varies across samples and plays a critical role in speculative decoding performance. Moreover, these values exhibit a clear local structure, concentrating around the training block size, which reduces the problem to a low-dimensional and structured decision space. Based on these insights, we propose BlockPilot, a sample-adaptive policy that predicts the optimal block size from the prefilling representation. Specifically, we formulate block size selection as a lightweight policy learning problem and propose an instance-adaptive decision mechanism that predicts the optimal block size based on the representation of the prefilling stage. The prediction is performed only once after prefilling, allowing for seamless integration. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method is plug-and-play, introduces minimal overhead, and consistently improves efficiency, achieving an acceptance length of 5.92 and a 4.20$\times$ speedup on Qwen3-4B under temperature $T=1$.

</details>


### [113] [Linguistic Bias Mitigation for Spoofing Detection via Gradient Reversal and A Variational Information Bottleneck](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31411)
*Anh-Tuan Dao,Driss Matrouf,Mickael Rouvier,Nicholas Evans*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces a linguistic-invariant framework for spoofing detection in voice biometrics using adversarial learning to address poor generalization in out-of-domain data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to improve the robustness of spoofing detection frameworks against generative speech technology by addressing the linguistic bias that hinders their generalization across different datasets.

Method: The method employs a linguistic-aware teacher model and a student detector guided by teacher-student adversarial learning with gradient reversal to minimize linguistic information. A Variational Information Bottleneck is added to preserve important non-linguistic features.

Result: The proposed framework achieves up to a 36.2% relative reduction in Equal Error Rate (EER) compared to baseline approaches across nine datasets.

Conclusion: The linguistic-invariant framework significantly improves the generalization ability of voice biometrics spoofing detectors, addressing the limitations posed by linguistic bias in training data.

Abstract: Rapid advancements in generative speech technology have compromised the reliability of voice biometrics. While current spoofing detectors excel when assessed under in-domain conditions, generalisation to out-of-domain settings is often poor. We show that this can be due to linguistic bias. A reliance on linguistic cues observed in training data can then compromise robustness to cross-data. We propose a linguistic-invariant spoofing detection framework utilizing teacher-student adversarial learning. The linguistic-aware teacher model, pre-trained on linguistic content of an external dataset, guides the student detector via gradient reversal to minimize the linguistic information. To prevent the inadvertent removal of non-linguistic cues, we incorporate a Variational Information Bottleneck to enable suppression of principal cues. Across nine DF Arena datasets, our method achieves up to a 36.2% relative reduction in the EER compare to the baseline.

</details>


### [114] [Clinically Structured Rank-Gated LoRA for Cross-Benchmark Medical Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31432)
*Yining Huang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces BiRG-LoRA, a rank-gated LoRA method for medical question answering, achieving superior accuracy across benchmarks using fewer parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for medical question answering struggle with heterogeneous knowledge domains and reasoning operations, requiring parameter-efficient solutions.

Method: BiRG-LoRA proposes a single-adapter rank-gated LoRA method that uses input-conditioned rank dimensions and biaxial gating to adapt to different medical question types efficiently.

Result: BiRG-LoRA achieves 69.31% accuracy averaged over four benchmarks with fewer trainable parameters, outperforming prior state-of-the-art methods like MoELoRA and vanilla LoRA.

Conclusion: BiRG-LoRA demonstrates that clinically structured rank allocation improves performance in cross-benchmark medical QA with parameter efficiency, though training-seed variance remains an area for further research.

Abstract: Medical multiple-choice question answering requires parameter-efficient adaptation across heterogeneous knowledge domains and reasoning operations. A medication question, a diagnostic decision, a public-health item, and a nursing-action item may require different low-rank updates, while some recall items should preserve the base model's representation with only mild adapter intervention. We propose BiRG-LoRA, a single-adapter rank-gated LoRA method for medical question answering. BiRG-LoRA keeps one LoRA module per target layer but makes its rank dimension input-conditioned: for each question, a biaxial gate combines hidden semantic evidence with specialty/profession priors, clinical-operation priors, and their interaction to select a sparse top-$k$ subset of rank atoms. A scalar injection coefficient further controls the strength of the selected adapter update. Under a matched Qwen3-8B CMB-source protocol, BiRG-LoRA achieves the highest four-benchmark macro-average accuracy among trainable PEFT baselines and matched routing controls: 69.31% averaged over CMB, CMExam, MedQA, and MedMCQA. It improves over MoELoRA by 0.89 percentage points while using 28.1% fewer trainable parameters; a paired, benchmark-stratified bootstrap over final predictions gives a 95% confidence interval of [0.42, 1.37] for this macro-average gain. Basic controls show that BiRG-LoRA also improves over vanilla LoRA r16 and active-rank-matched LoRA r4 by 0.83 macro points, and an evaluation-time weak-axis perturbation check suggests that performance is not brittle to moderate tag noise. The results support a bounded claim: clinically structured rank allocation improves cross-benchmark medical QA under a matched single-seed protocol, while training-seed variance remains future work.

</details>


### [115] [Revising RVL-CDIP: Quantifying Errors and Test-Train Overlap](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31446)
*Stefan Larson,Attila Nagy,Sam Desai,Cyrus Desai,Nicole C. Lima,Yixin Yuan,Siddharth Betala,Kaushal K. Prajapati,Jamiu T. Suleiman,Sharad Duwal,Kevin Leach*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper identifies significant issues in the RVL-CDIP dataset, including label errors and test-train data overlap, and proposes fixes, resulting in performance changes and improved out-of-distribution generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address labeling errors and dataset contamination in the RVL-CDIP dataset, which may compromise model evaluation reliability and performance benchmarking.

Method: They identify and correct label errors and test-train overlap in the RVL-CDIP dataset, create variations with these fixes, and benchmark model performance on these modified datasets.

Result: Analysis reveals 12% label errors and 35% test-train data duplication in the dataset. Correcting label errors improves classification accuracy, while removing duplicates often reduces it. Training on corrected data improves out-of-distribution performance substantially, with gains averaging 8.1 percentage points and reaching up to 14 percentage points.

Conclusion: Fixing label errors and addressing dataset overlap can enhance dataset reliability and improve generalization in document classification models, particularly in out-of-distribution scenarios.

Abstract: RVL-CDIP is a popular dataset for benchmarking document classifiers. However, the dataset contains ample amounts of label errors as well as non-trivial amounts of test-train overlap, both of which may impact model performance metrics. In this paper, we address these two problems by (1) finding and fixing label errors, and (2) detecting and addressing test-train overlap. We produce several variations of RVL-CDIP with label error and test-train overlap fixes, and benchmark document classification performance on these new RVL-CDIP variations. Our rigorous analysis of RVL-CDIP finds that the corpus contains 12\% label error and approximately 35% test-train duplication. Remediation sees improvements in classification accuracy when errors are removed, but sees decreases in accuracy when duplicates are removed. We additionally evaluate models on RVL-CDIP-N, an out-of-distribution benchmark, finding that training on error-corrected data substantially improves OOD generalization, with supervised models gaining an average of 8.1 percentage points in accuracy and improvements as large as 14 percentage points.

</details>


### [116] [Team MKC at CLPsych 2026: Capturing and Characterizing Mental Health Changes through Social Media Timeline Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31464)
*Kyomin Hwang,Hyeonjin Kim,Hyunho Lee,Nojun Kwak*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This study proposes an LLM-based pipeline to analyze mental health using sequential user posts with a unified framework for both post-level and temporal user-level modeling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The rising prevalence of mental health disorders and lack of professional care accessibility motivate the need for scalable computational tools for early detection and monitoring.

Method: The paper suggests a pipeline leveraging Large Language Models (LLMs) to analyze mental health by integrating post-level assessment with temporal user-level modeling in online posts.

Result: The proposed pipeline contributes to mental health analysis by improving holistic understanding through combined post- and user-level modeling.

Conclusion: With the proposed LLM-based pipeline, more effective and scalable tools for monitoring psychological well-being can be developed, assisting in mental health analysis.

Abstract: Recent advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) have motivated their adoption across a wide range of domains, including Artificial Intelligence (AI) for mental health. Given the growing prevalence of mental health disorders worldwide and the limited accessibility of professional care, there is an increasing demand for scalable computational approaches that can assist in early detection and continuous monitoring of psychological well-being. In this area, ongoing efforts have focused on curating domain-specific datasets and leveraging them to develop LLMs capable of supporting holistic mental health analysis. In line with this direction, we propose an LLM-based pipeline for comprehensive mental health analysis over sequentially ordered user posts, as part of the CLPsych shared task. Our pipeline offers a unified framework that jointly enables post-level assessment and user-level temporal modeling.

</details>


### [117] [Building an ASR Solution for Training and Assessing Children's Reading](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31508)
*Yacouba Diarra,Nouhoum Souleymane Coulibaly,Mamadou Dembele,Aymane Dembele,Michael Leventhal*

Main category: cs.CL
