Motivation: To address inefficiencies in edge-cloud inference systems where weaker models at the edge are forced to run computation even when offloading frames to the cloud would be more optimal.

Method: The paper introduces a lightweight weak-skipping estimator for routing decision-making and a budget-adaptive routing strategy that adjusts based on offload budget thresholds.

Result: The proposed system improves accuracy and efficiency, reducing per-frame latency by up to 19.1 ms, and even surpasses the strong model's peak mAP in certain settings.

Conclusion: The budget-adaptive routing framework is a superior alternative, offering both computational savings and increased effectiveness, thereby outperforming state-of-the-art systems.

Abstract: Edge-cloud inference collaborations are often designed with a routing estimator that decides whether to offload each frame from weak models at the edge to stronger models in the cloud. Existing systems place the routing estimator after the weak detector, so the weak forward pass still runs even on frames that are later offloaded. In this paper, we argue that this weak-conditioned design can be suboptimal when the offload budget varies. First, we present a competitive weak-skipping estimator (0.153 GFLOPs, about 29x lighter than the weak detector at 4.49 GFLOPs) that extracts routing signal from raw pixels, outperforming the common after-weak placement weak-conditioned baselines. Second, we show that neither weak-skipping nor weak-conditioned placement dominates across the full operating curve, and we propose budget-adaptive routing, which selects between them by offload budget via two offline-tuned thresholds. On PASCAL VOC, our budget-adaptive router traces the upper accuracy envelope of both fixed placements across the operating range. Our method reduces per-frame latency by up to 19.1 ms (about 30% lower at rho = 0.9). Besides outperforming SOTA methods, it is surprisingly stronger than the strong model (+1.7 pp over the strong model's peak mAP) at some operating points with far less compute. Artifacts are available at https://github.com/ViGeng/bgt-ada

</details>


### [552] [Semantic Leakage and Privacy Preservation in Relay-Assisted Semantic Communications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31973)
*Yalin E. Sagduyu,Tugba Erpek,Aylin Yener,Sennur Ulukus*

Main category: cs.NI

TL;DR: The paper examines privacy vulnerabilities in relay-assisted semantic communication systems and proposes an iterative adversarial training framework to minimize semantic leakage while maintaining communication performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Semantic communication prioritizes transmitting task-relevant information efficiently but faces privacy risks, particularly when relay nodes infer semantic meaning from latent data representations.

Method: An iterative adversarial training framework alternates between optimizing the relay's eavesdropping function and improving the legitimate system, degrading semantic inference at the relay while preserving decoding performance at the intended receiver.

Result: The method significantly enlarges the gap in semantic accuracy between the intended receiver and the relay eavesdropper under various channel conditions, maintaining high reconstruction fidelity while suppressing semantic leakage.

Conclusion: The proposed framework provides stealthy and effective privacy protection in semantic communication without compromising communication quality.

Abstract: Semantic communication (SemCom) has emerged as a promising paradigm in which the transmission of task-relevant information is prioritized over raw data, enabling efficient and robust communication under resource and channel constraints. In this paper, the privacy implications of relay-assisted SemCom systems are studied, where the intermediate relay node operates directly on learned latent representations. It is shown that the relay, even without access to source data, can reliably infer semantic meaning and reconstruct signals with performance comparable to that of the legitimate receiver, revealing a fundamental privacy vulnerability of semantic representations. To address this issue, an iterative adversarial training framework is proposed in which a strong, adaptively trained eavesdropper at the relay is explicitly accounted for. The proposed approach alternates between optimizing the relay's eavesdropping function and the legitimate system, resulting in representations that preserve semantic decoding performance at the intended receiver while degrading semantic inference at the relay. The semantic accuracy gap between the legitimate receiver and the eavesdropper is significantly enlarged across channel conditions. Importantly, this protection is achieved in a stealthy manner, with high reconstruction fidelity maintained while semantic leakage is selectively suppressed.

</details>


### [553] [Semantic-Aware Multiple Access via Spatial Redundancy Exploitation for Uplink-Dominant 6G Use Cases](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31715)
*Hamidreza Mazandarani,Masoud Shokrnezhad,Tarik Taleb,Onur Günlü*

Main category: cs.NI

TL;DR: This paper introduces a semantic-aware multiple access scheme for efficient 6G uplink transmissions in vehicular networks by reducing spatial redundancy across users' observations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 6G use cases like cooperative vehicular streaming demand efficient uplink transmission of large-scale visual data over limited wireless resources, necessitating new methods to minimize redundancy and optimize communication.

Method: The authors propose a two-phase method: (1) vehicles share observation patches via V2V links to compute spatial redundancy and (2) only semantically important, non-redundant patches are transmitted to the base station for reconstruction.

Result: Simulation results in dense urban vehicular scenarios show improved high-fidelity reconstruction for a larger proportion of users, demonstrating a significant reduction in redundant transmissions.

Conclusion: Semantic-aware multiple access schemes are promising for achieving resource-efficient and sustainable uplink communication in emerging 6G systems, particularly in scenarios with high spatial redundancy across users.

Abstract: Emerging uplink-dominant 6G use cases, such as cooperative vehicular streaming, require efficient transmission of high-volume visual data over limited wireless resources. While semantic communications can reduce traffic by prioritizing task-relevant content, most existing approaches treat users independently and therefore overlook spatial redundancy among nearby devices' observations. This paper proposes a semantic-aware multiple access scheme that exploits overlapping fields of view among vehicular users to reduce redundant uplink transmissions. We formulate a joint perception and transmission control problem in which users decide which image patches to transmit, when to transmit them, and over which channel, subject to communication constraints. To address the resulting complexity, we introduce a practical two-phase approach. First, nearby vehicles share selected observation patches over Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) links to calculate inter-user spatial redundancy. Second, users transmit only semantically important, non-redundant patches to the base station, where observations can be reconstructed using the received patches and complementary views from neighboring vehicles. Simulation results in a dense urban vehicular scenario demonstrate that our approach improves the proportion of users who achieve high-fidelity reconstruction, highlighting the potential of semantic-aware multiple access for sustainable and resource-efficient 6G uplink systems.

</details>


<div id='cs.MM'></div>

# cs.MM [[Back]](#toc)

### [554] [Evidence Triangulation for Multimodal Fact-Checking in the Wild](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31367)
*Stefanos-Iordanis Papadopoulos,Zacharias Chrysidis,Christos Koutlis,Symeon Papadopoulos,Panagiotis C. Petrantonakis*

Main category: cs.MM

TL;DR: This paper presents X-POSE, a new benchmark for fact-checking multimodal posts, and TRENT, a model leveraging cross-attention and relational fusion, achieving superior results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations in multimodal misinformation detection, such as reliance on synthetic data and ineffective fusion methods, and improve accuracy on real-world, complex social media misinformation.

Method: Introduce X-POSE, a real-world benchmark, and TRENT, a model using three parallel cross-attention streams with relational fusion to analyze posts and external evidence.

Result: TRENT outperforms current state-of-the-art models and commercial visual language models in detecting multimodal misinformation.

Conclusion: X-POSE and TRENT together advance multimodal misinformation detection, with public resources provided to encourage further research.

Abstract: The proliferation of multimedia content on social platforms has fueled multimodal misinformation, where images are used to reinforce false claims. Consequently, Multimodal Fact-Checking (MFC) has emerged as an increasingly important research area. However, current progress is hindered by a reliance on synthetic training data and curated benchmarks that fail to capture the complexity of in-the-wild data. Furthermore, existing detection models rely on restricted intra-modality consistency or unconstrained all-to-all fusion, failing to capture nuanced relations between posts and external evidence. To address these limitations, we introduce X-POSE, a benchmark of real-world, community-annotated multimodal posts from X (formerly Twitter), augmented with full-length news articles retrieved via VLM-optimized search. Additionally, we propose TRENT, a novel MFC model that performs evidence triangulation using three parallel cross-attention streams alongside a relational fusion mechanism that explicitly models entailment and contradiction. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that TRENT consistently outperforms state-of-the-art specialized models and commercial VLMs. The code, prompt templates, and dataset are available at https://github.com/stevejpapad/evidence-triangulation

</details>


<div id='math.ST'></div>

# math.ST [[Back]](#toc)

### [555] [On Optimal Data Splitting for Split Conformal Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31600)
*Sayan Das,Bahram Yaghooti,Todd A. Kuffner,Soumendra N. Lahiri*

Main category: math.ST

TL;DR: The paper provides a theoretical framework to optimize data splitting in split conformal prediction, aiming to minimize prediction interval length while maintaining finite-sample coverage guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite the significance of data splitting in split conformal prediction for constructing efficient prediction intervals, the method to minimize interval length while ensuring coverage was not well-characterized.

Method: The authors derived analytical characterizations of the length-optimal split ratio in general cases and specialized their findings to regression settings like linear regression, nonparametric regression, and neural networks. Additionally, they proposed a data-driven method to determine the optimal proportion.

Result: The framework revealed how model-related aspects influence optimal data splitting and provided practical guidance for creating shorter prediction intervals. Experiments verified its effectiveness on synthetic and real-world datasets.

Conclusion: The study offers a principled approach for optimizing data splitting in split conformal prediction, improving prediction efficiency without sacrificing coverage, and has broad applicability in practical scenarios.

Abstract: Conformal prediction and its variants, including the split conformal prediction, provide a distribution-free framework for uncertainty quantification by constructing prediction intervals or sets with finite-sample coverage guarantees. The statistical efficiency of these intervals depends critically on how the data are split into training and calibration samples. Despite its practical importance, a principled characterization of the training-calibration split that minimizes prediction interval length while maintaining coverage has remained largely unresolved. In this paper, we develop a theoretical framework for optimal data splitting in split conformal prediction. We first analyze the problem in a general setting and derive analytical characterizations of the length-optimal split ratio under both symmetric and asymmetric regimes. We then show how the general results specialize to several commonly used regression settings, including linear regression, nonparametric regression, and neural networks, thereby demonstrating the scope of the framework. We also describe a data-based method for selecting the optimal proportion. Our analysis clarifies how model-related features govern the optimal allocation of samples between training and calibration and provides principled guidance for constructing shorter prediction intervals. Experiments on both synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate the applicability of the proposed methodology across a variety of practical scenarios.

</details>


<div id='physics.ao-ph'></div>

# physics.ao-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [556] [Conditional Tropical Cyclogenesis Rates via Rare-Event Sampling in a Neural Weather Emulator](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30920)
*John S. Schreck,William Chapman,Charlie Becker,David John Gagne*

Main category: physics.ao-ph

TL;DR: This study uses Forward Flux Sampling (FFS) with a neural weather emulator to estimate how often tropical cyclones intensify to hurricane levels under various conditions, achieving significant computational efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurately predicting tropical cyclone genesis rates is challenging due to their variability and the computational limitations of direct ensemble sampling methods.

Method: The authors paired Forward Flux Sampling (FFS), a statistical rare-event technique, with a neural weather emulator (SDL-WXFormer). FFS breaks down cyclone intensification into sequential probabilities, leveraging the emulator for computationally feasible simulations.

Result: FFS successfully resolved genesis rates over three orders of magnitude for Atlantic weather in 2022, producing results consistent with observations and offering computational enhancements ranging from 3X to 140X.

Conclusion: This approach enables efficient, detailed study of tropical cyclone genesis and intensification, with potential for further application using higher-resolution emulators.

Abstract: We couple Forward Flux Sampling (FFS), a non-equilibrium rare-event technique from statistical mechanics, to a neural weather emulator (SDL-WXFormer, 1° grid spacing) to estimate conditional tropical cyclogenesis rates, or how often a tropical cyclone achieves a hurricane-level central pressure, without modifying model dynamics. Tropical cyclogenesis rates vary by orders of magnitude across regimes, yet direct ensemble sampling cannot resolve this variability at operationally feasible ensemble sizes. FFS decomposes the rare disturbance to mature cyclone intensification path into a flux through an initial interface pressure and a product of conditional crossing probabilities across four intermediate interface pressures. We use the 1° emulator because FFS requires O(10^4) model trajectories per initial condition, and because the model's calibrated stochastic layers provide the necessary exploratory spread. Applied to 98 Atlantic basin initial conditions spanning 21 August - 8 October 2022, FFS resolves genesis rates spanning nearly three orders of magnitude, capturing a seasonal cycle qualitatively consistent with observations. A self-consistency check comparing FFS rates to independent direct-sampling rates yields a mean ratio of 1.03 +/- 0.15 across all initial conditions. Computational enhancement factors range from 3X (most active environment) to 140X (most suppressed), with a geometric mean of 14X. Three case studies illustrate the physical diagnostics the method provides: the rate-limiting step is initial tropical organization for the Earl environment, uniformly high crossing probabilities for the Fiona precursor environment, and a compound barrier at the final intensification stages for the Ian environment. More efficient emulators would enable application of FFS to finer resolutions.

</details>


### [557] [Scaling Storm-Resolving Atmospheric AI Simulation to the Entire Planet](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31248)
*Zeyuan Hu,Akshay Subramaniam,Noel Keen,Tao Ge,Jaideep Pathak,Mohammad Shoaib Abbas,Suman Ravuri,Karthik Kashinath,Naser Mahfouz,Peter Caldwell,Mike Pritchard,Noah Brenowitz*

Main category: physics.ao-ph

TL;DR: STRATA, a novel AI emulator for global storm-resolving atmospheric dynamics, achieves 50x better energy efficiency than traditional models by employing autoregressive architecture and innovative techniques.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of global atmospheric models that lack the resolution to resolve kilometer-scale convective systems and the high computational cost of storm-resolving physics-based models.

Method: Introduced STRATA, an AI model trained on high-resolution atmospheric data using techniques like local 3D attention, Stereographic Rotary Position Embedding, and overlapping-tile blending for autoregressive dynamics emulation.

Result: STRATA delivers stable 24-hour global rollouts with realistic dynamics, achieving 48 simulated days per megawatt-hour and significant energy and time efficiency on GPUs.

Conclusion: STRATA demonstrates the feasibility of energy-efficient kilometer-scale atmospheric emulation with AI, making it a promising alternative for high-resolution climate modeling.

Abstract: Kilometer-scale convection shapes precipitation extremes, tropical organization, and cloud feedbacks, but most global atmospheric models approximate these processes at 25-100 km resolution. Global storm-resolving physics models resolve convective systems explicitly, but at a cost -- roughly one MWh per simulated day on exascale supercomputers -- that limits long-duration simulation. We introduce STRATA (Storm-resolving Tile-based autoRegressive Atmosphere Transformer Architecture), the first autoregressive AI emulator for global storm-resolving atmospheric dynamics. STRATA is trained on the highest-resolution atmospheric dataset yet used for global AI emulation: 17 days of SCREAM physics-model output at 4.9-km resolution (~25 million grid cells) sampled every 10 minutes. Our central premise is that on 10-minute timescales atmospheric dynamics are predominantly local, so training on small spatial tiles trades scarce global temporal samples for abundant local spatial samples and enables global rollout via overlapping-tile blending. STRATA combines 3D patch embedding and local 3D neighborhood attention, a novel Stereographic Rotary Position Embedding (StereoRoPE) for grid-invariant encoding, and a pixel-space de-aliasing decoder that suppresses patch-scale rollout artifacts. An iso-FLOP scaling study reveals that km-scale emulation requires ~10x more FLOPs per grid point than coarse-resolution AI weather models, consistent with the higher information density of convective-scale dynamics. Trained on only 17 days of data, STRATA produces stable 24-hour global rollouts with realistic km-scale dynamics across diverse regimes, though large-scale biases develop with lead time. It achieves 48 simulation days per megawatt-hour -- about 50 times better energy efficiency than the SCREAM physics model -- and 741 simulated days per wall-clock day at 512 H100 GPUs. Code and dataset are publicly available.

</details>


<div id='eess.SY'></div>

# eess.SY [[Back]](#toc)

### [558] [A Systematic Approach to Multi-Agent AI from Advanced Regulatory Control Theory: Safe and Auditable LLM Operator Agents for Process Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30877)
*Idelfonso B. R. Nogueira,Sigurd Skogestad*

Main category: eess.SY

TL;DR: The paper introduces a multi-agent system based on control theory that uses specialized Large Language Model (LLM) agents for domain-specific tasks, evaluated successfully on a dairy-barn ventilation case.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs struggle with domain-specific tasks due to difficulty in supplying narrow contexts and bounding tasks. Using control theory principles could address these issues by decomposing tasks into specialized agents.

Method: The multi-agent system maps control principles (e.g., Advanced Regulatory Control theory) to specialized LLM operator agents and an orchestrator agent. Two orchestrator variants are tested: deterministic rules and a Claude-based slower-tier LLM.

Result: The system was tested on a dairy-barn ventilation case, showing it can produce auditable control trajectories paired with rationales, showcasing practical usability on consumer hardware at a manageable cadence.

Conclusion: Multi-agent systems using control theory principles show promise for domain-specific tasks, ensuring task scope limits and deterministic resolution of conflicts in multi-agent interactions.

Abstract: Recent literature shows that large language models (LLMs) are useful for general-purpose tasks yet perform poorly on specific domain ones. One reason is the difficulty of supplying narrow context to a general-purpose model and of bounding the task it is asked to perform. It is possible to hypothesise that a multi-agent reformulation under process-control principles offers a route to address those points, since control theory provides a discipline of decomposing a system into elements of contained scope, each defending one controlled variable, with conflicts resolved by structural priority: MIN/MAX selector networks for CV-CV switching and split-range (split-parallel) logic for MV-MV switching. The present work proposes such a reformulation, derived from Advanced Regulatory Control (ARC) theory. Each feedback loop in the ARC chain is mapped to one specialised LLM operator agent carrying the loop's control-theoretic context (controlled variable, setpoint, chain priority, selector kind). The chain's interaction logic (MIN/MAX selectors, override paths) is encapsulated as a single orchestrator agent. Two orchestrator variants are tested: a deterministic rule chain, and a Claude-based LLM orchestrator at a slower tier. The control principles limit each agent's task and inform how its limitations are handled. The multi-agent system inherits the safety property of the ARC chain: every constraint conflict is resolved deterministically by the orchestrator, regardless of the LLM output. Evaluated on a dairy-barn ventilation case over a 4-day mixed-season scenario, Qwen 2.5 7B Instruct operator agents running offline on a 24 GB consumer GPU at a 5-minute cadence produce auditable trajectories, each paired with an operator-voice rationale that supports a control campaign logbook.

</details>


### [559] [ShardNet: Training Neural Controllers with Hard, Non-Convex Constraints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30935)
*Long Kiu Chung,Shreyas Kousik*

Main category: eess.SY

TL;DR: The paper introduces ShardNet, a novel neural network ensuring strict safety constraints by embedding constraints into its structure, outperforming existing methods in both safety and performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of deploying neural network control policies on safety-critical systems while ensuring strict safety constraints, as existing methods struggle with balancing safety and performance optimization.

Method: The authors developed ShardNet, which embeds unions of polyhedral safety constraints within the neural network architecture using a differentiable projection layer parameterized by a classification network. Additionally, they proposed a value function training and verification method for ReLU networks.

Result: ShardNet demonstrated 100% safety on benchmark sets and significantly reduced objective loss compared to existing formal methods. Its value function training technique also generated safe sets three times larger than existing approaches.

Conclusion: The proposed ShardNet ensures strict safety in neural network controllers while optimizing performance independently, paving the way for safer and more efficient deployment in safety-critical systems.

Abstract: While neural network control policies are powerful, their deployment on safety critical systems depends on ensuring that they obey strict constraints. Existing work often treats safety as a metric to optimize for, which competes with other performance objectives, if training converges at all. Instead, we introduce ShardNet, a neural network architecture that strictly enforces unions of polyhedral constraints by construction, using a differentiable projection layer parameterized by a classification network. The key insight is to embed safety into the neural network's structure, allowing performance to be optimized independently because formal safety guarantees are always given. In contrast with existing neural architectures that can only enforce simple convex constraints, ShardNet enables the first safe-by-construction synthesis of forward-invariant neural network controllers on closed-loop systems where safety constraints are expressed as nonconvex unions of polyhedras or learned value function level sets. To support this, we also introduce a technique to verify and train such value functions correctly as rectified linear unit (ReLU) networks, which has not previously been possible. On double integrator benchmarks drawn from the literature, ShardNet policies maintain 100% safety on verified sets and achieves significantly lower objective loss compared to existing formal methods. Furthermore, our value function training technique also produces safe sets more than 3 times larger than existing verification approaches.

</details>


### [560] [Automating Cause-Effect Specification with Knowledge Graphs and Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31614)
*Javal Vyas,Milapji Singh Gill,Mehmet Mercangöz*

Main category: eess.SY

TL;DR: The paper introduces a semantic-AI framework integrating a Knowledge Graph (KG) and a constrained Large Language Model (LLM) to optimize the creation of engineering safety specifications like cause-and-effect (C&E) matrices.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the inefficiencies, manual effort, and inconsistencies in the creation of engineering specifications essential to process control and safety.

Method: A combined approach using a Knowledge Graph (KG) for machine-interpretable representation and a constrained Large Language Model (LLM) to generate operator-ready safety narratives and rules, adhering to strict semantic constraints.

Result: The framework successfully demonstrates on a modular process plant how engineering semantics, diagnostic relations, and machine-verifiable specifications can be generated efficiently.

Conclusion: The semantic-AI framework automates and enhances the creation of safety specifications, reducing manual effort and improving consistency, thus advancing process safety and control.

Abstract: Engineering specifications such as interlocks, alarm rationalization tables, and cause-and-effect (C&E) matrices remain central to process control and safety, yet their creation is still predominantly manual, document-driven, and prone to inconsistency. This paper presents a semantic-AI framework that automates the generation of C&E logic by combining a knowledge graph (KG) with a constrained large language model (LLM) layer. The KG builds on an established modular alignment ontology to represent process structure, operating modes, faults, symptoms, causes, and mitigation actions in a machine-interpretable form. The LLM then transforms this information into operator-ready safety narratives and Semantic Web Rule Language (SWRL) rules under strict ontology and vocabulary constraints, grounding the generated artifacts in the underlying semantic model. The workflow is demonstrated on a modular process plant, showing how engineering semantics, diagnostic relations, and machine-verifiable specifications can be generated from a unified knowledge representation with reduced manual effort.

</details>


### [561] [A Tutorial on Autonomous Fault-Tolerant Control Using Knowledge-Grounded LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31635)
*Javal Vyas,Milapji Singh Gill,Artan Markaj,Felix Gehlhoff,Mehmet Mercangöz*

Main category: eess.SY

TL;DR: This paper explores using Large Language Model (LLM) agents as supervisory planners to aid fault recovery decisions in process plants, focusing on safe operating modes while avoiding shutdowns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve operator-dependent fault recovery in process plants by leveraging LLM agents for automated and safe decision support.

Method: The framework treats LLMs as constrained planners, incorporates plant-specific knowledge for action proposals, and verifies proposals using external validators (symbolic or simulation-based).

Result: Developed three design dimensions (recovery patterns, validation strategies, deployment constraints) and provided Python environments for case study implementation in modular processes.

Conclusion: LLM agents, when combined with thorough validation and integration, can enhance fault recovery decision-making in chemical processes, potentially reducing operator dependency and enhancing safety.

Abstract: Fault recovery in process plants still relies heavily on plant operators, especially when faults fall outside predefined supervisory logic. Operators interpret alarms, procedures, P\&IDs, interlocks, and process trends, then decide how to move the plant to a safe operating mode without triggering a shutdown. This paper examines how Large Language Model (LLM) agents can support such recovery decisions. The proposed framework treats the LLM as a constrained supervisory planner. It uses plant-specific knowledge to propose recovery actions, and every proposal is checked by an external validator (symbolic or simulation-based) before actuation. The paper develops three design dimensions for applying the framework: the recovery patterns for which LLM agents are useful, the validation strategies that separate admissible from inadmissible proposals, and the deployment constraints imposed by latency, knowledge engineering, safety integration, and model lifecycle management. To make the framework directly usable, two openly available executable Python environments are provided. Both re-implement established case studies, a modular mixing module and a continuous stirred-tank reactor, extended with configurable faults and defined interfaces for custom recovery and validation methods.

</details>


<div id='cs.CR'></div>

# cs.CR [[Back]](#toc)

### [562] [Exploring Side-Channel Protections in Hardware Implementations of PQC ML-KEM Verification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31681)
*Davis Ranney,Yashaswini I Makaram,A. Adam Ding,Yunsi Fei*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper investigates side-channel vulnerabilities in FPGA-based and microcontroller implementations of Fujisaki-Okamoto (FO) verification in ML-KEM, highlighting leakage issues even in protected designs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the critical need for resilience against physical side-channel attacks as ML-KEM becomes a post-quantum cryptographic standard.

Method: Evaluate three verification implementations (unprotected, hash-based, and higher-order masked) on both microcontroller and FPGA platforms to compare side-channel resilience.

Result: FPGAs, despite their benefits, show stronger side-channel leakage in parallelized settings, and even higher-order masked designs can lead to full secret-key recovery due to hardware effects.

Conclusion: Performance-constrained and parallelized hardware environments face persistent challenges in securing post-quantum cryptographic algorithms against side-channel attacks.

Abstract: As ML-KEM is adopted as a post-quantum cryptographic standard, resilience against physical side-channel attacks has become essential. Among the constituent steps, the decapsulation Fujisaki-Okamoto (FO) verification is particularly vulnerable to side-channel power and electromagnetic (EM) analysis. In this work, we focus on common FPGA-based implementations and examine their side-channel vulnerabilities, and compare them with those of microcontroller implementations. Three verification implementations, unprotected, hash-based (first-order), and higher-order masked, are evaluated for side-channel security on both a microcontroller and an FPGA. While FPGAs offer higher speed and parallelism, they often exhibit stronger side-channel leakage, especially in high bandwidth configurations. The higher-order masked designs still leak information about the underlying data due to hardware-level effects and data-dependent processing. Our experiments show that their parallelized processing on FPGAs introduces sufficient first-order leakage for full secret-key recovery. These results underscore the persistent challenge of securing PQC algorithms in performance-constrained and parallelized hardware environments.

</details>


### [563] [The Decomposition Is the Fingerprint: Per-Component Identity for Agent Skills](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31272)
*Hongliang Liu,Yuhao Wu,Tung-Ling Li*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces a method for generating compact, locality-sensitive fingerprints for AI skill identification based on their components (prompt, code, tools), enabling efficient and detailed skill recognition.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The need to manage AI skills, which increasingly involve runtime acquisition and execution, requires a stable identity mechanism to differentiate between variations, tampering, and independent creations while preserving similarities.

Method: The authors propose a 120-byte fingerprint generated using a per-component triple (prompt, code, tools) embedding technique with multi-bank SimHash, compared through Hamming distance.

Result: The fingerprint demonstrates high accuracy with an AUC of 0.974, recognizes tampered copies of skills, and localizes changes while using 77x fewer bits than the original embeddings it approximates.

Conclusion: The fingerprint serves as a compact identity signal for AI skills, supporting classification, family grouping, novelty detection, and registry formation, but remains distinct from a behavioral safety verdict, complementing verification processes.

Abstract: AI agents increasingly acquire and execute skills at runtime: bundles of prompt instructions, executable code, and tool declarations fetched from marketplaces and other agents. Governing them needs a stable notion of skill identity, yet cryptographic hashing is engineered to destroy the very similarity we need, as a one-character edit scrambles the digest. We present a compact, locality-sensitive fingerprint that embeds each component of a skill and projects it to bits with a multi-bank SimHash, giving a fixed 120-byte signature compared in constant time by Hamming distance. Our central claim is that keeping the fingerprint as a per-component triple (prompt, code, tools), rather than a single score, is what makes it useful: the triple recovers skill-family identity through paraphrase, renaming, refactoring, and controlled code translation when another component remains shared, while independent multilingual reimplementation is not recovered; it also localizes which component carries the reuse. We claim lineage, not behavioral equivalence: identity supplies the structural axis of a registry and leaves safety to behavioral verification. The fingerprint reaches an area under the ROC curve (AUC) of 0.974 (95% CI [0.956, 0.994]) over 4,950 pairwise comparisons while using 77x fewer bits than the embedding it approximates, with ranking preserved in expectation and finite-bit concentration; the per-component split turns one number into relationship classification, families, novelty, and a portable "SkillBOM" for a skill registry. On a 906-skill injection benchmark the fingerprint recognizes injected skills as tampered copies of a known base and localizes the change, but recognition is not trust: it remains, by design, an identity signal complementary to behavioral verification rather than a safety verdict.

</details>


### [564] [An AI-Based Solution for Secure Service Provisioning in IoT](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30701)
*Marco Arazzi,Mert Cihangiroglu,Serena Nicolazzo,Antonino Nocera,Vinod P*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper proposes a scalable security framework for IoT devices using DRL for adaptability and FL for behavioral analysis, ensuring secure and reliable service provisioning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address growing security challenges in IoT ecosystems by focusing on secure, adaptable, and reliable service provisioning.

Method: The framework combines Deep Reinforcement Learning for environment adaptability and Federated Learning for behavioral monitoring, incorporating a reliability score into the service provisioning process.

Result: Experimental evaluations show the framework is robust, scalable, and suitable for deployment on resource-constrained IoT devices.

Conclusion: The framework enhances IoT security and reliability by enabling smart objects to select service providers based on both functional and security reliability, proving its practicality for modern IoT applications.

Abstract: As the Internet of Things (IoT) continues its rapid expansion, the attack surface grows accordingly, with emerging threats targeting smart objects and their interactions. In this evolving landscape, securing service provisioning is crucial to ensure the proper functioning, security, and reliability of the IoT ecosystem. Service provisioning encompasses key tasks such as device registration, configuration, authentication, authorization, and software deployment, all of which are essential for seamless and secure IoT operations. In this paper, we present a comprehensive framework designed to select the most suitable smart objects to deliver a target service within a given IoT environment while also monitoring the behavior of the entities involved during the service provisioning phase. To achieve this, we employ a Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) approach in which an intelligent agent learns, through interaction with a complex, dynamic environment, how to adapt to changes while adhering to predefined security constraints. For behavioral monitoring, we leverage Federated Learning (FL) to develop a global Behavioral Fingerprinting (BF) model that is fully distributed and can analyze how IoT devices interact within the network. In addition, the BF is used to compute a reliability score for each service provider, reflecting its degree of compliance with the defined security constraints. This score is then incorporated into the service provisioning process, allowing smart objects to select providers not only according to functional suitability but also to their reliability level. Finally, we conduct an extensive experimental evaluation to assess the robustness and scalability of our approach. The results demonstrate that our solution can be effectively deployed even on resource-constrained IoT devices, making it a viable and scalable security-enhancing mechanism for modern IoT ecosystems.

</details>


### [565] [Understanding and Evaluating Claw-like Agent Security Through a Computer-Systems Lens](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30755)
*Peizhi Niu,Wenjie Qu,Shangding Gu,Tianneng Shi,Yuankai Li,Ahmad Tawaha,Hend Alzahrani,Vincent Siu,Boyi Li,Chenguang Wang,Jiaheng Zhang,Basel Alomair,Ming Jin,Muhao Chen,Chi Wang,Costas Spanos,Dawn Song*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper introduces SafeClawArena, a benchmark for assessing security vulnerabilities in Claw-like AI agents by testing them on adversarial tasks. The study reveals significant security weaknesses in existing systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation stems from the security implications of Claw-like agents, which handle high-level system responsibilities and persistent resources, but lack robust protection mechanisms compared to traditional systems.

Method: The authors design the SafeClawArena benchmark, consisting of 406 adversarial tasks across four attack categories. They test real agent platforms and LLMs using containerized environments and automated taint tracking for evaluation.

Result: They find high attack success rates (up to 70%, and 100% for malicious Plugins) across various platforms and LLMs. SeClaw reduces attack rates to 22% for GPT-5.4, while Claude-Opus-4.6 shows consistently low vulnerability rates.

Conclusion: The study highlights critical security gaps in Claw-like AI agents, showing the inadequacy of current defenses. It recommends improving security mechanisms and trade-offs for future systems hardening.

Abstract: Claw-like AI agents (e.g., OpenClaw) are always-on processes with persistent access to credentials, files, tools, and external services. They take on system-level responsibilities -- installing packages, maintaining state, scheduling subtasks, and mediating I/O -- making security failures far more severe than in other agents. Yet existing benchmarks focus on model responses and tool calls, leaving cross-component failure modes largely unmeasured. We adopt a computer-system analogy: treating a Claw-like agent as an agentic computer system whose gateway runtime plays an OS-like mediation role, whose Skills resemble user-installed applications, and whose Plugins resemble loadable extensions with runtime privileges. Each component has a classical counterpart whose protection mechanisms -- refined over decades of cybersecurity research -- are absent on the agent side. From this perspective, we develop SafeClawArena, a benchmark of 406 adversarial tasks across four attack surfaces (Skill Supply-Chain Integrity, Persistent State Exploitation, Cross-Boundary Data Flow, and Indirect Prompt Injection), executed in containerized replicas of real agent platforms with canary-marked credentials and evaluated via automated taint tracking across nine output channels. We evaluate three platforms (OpenClaw, NemoClaw, SeClaw) and five frontier LLMs. The highest attack success rate reaches 70%; malicious Plugins succeed in 100% of cases regardless of the LLM. SeClaw cuts GPT-5.4's attack success rate from 70% to 22%, partly through utility-security tradeoffs rather than active defenses, while Claude-Opus-4.6 already sits near a 22% floor on every platform. These results expose the inadequacy of current defenses and suggest directions for future hardening. Code and data: https://github.com/sunblaze-ucb/SafeClawArena.

</details>


### [566] [Security--Fidelity Tradeoffs: The Hidden Cost of Prompt Injection Defense](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30783)
*Mitchell Hermon,Rahul Gupta,Weitong Ruan,Ekraam Sabir,Haohan Wang*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces SecFid, a benchmark to measure the tradeoff between security and fidelity in defending language models against indirect prompt injection. It highlights that no defense achieves both security and fidelity comprehensively.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation stems from identifying a gap in current metrics which overlook the fidelity aspect when assessing robustness against indirect prompt injection attacks in LLMs.

Method: The authors propose SecFid, a benchmark designed to measure both security and fidelity by ensuring that injection execution, faithful processing as data, and ignorance produce distinguishable outputs.

Result: Through their benchmark, they find that achieving both security and fidelity simultaneously is unattainable—models and defenses either compromise fidelity for security or vice versa.

Conclusion: Fidelity should be considered alongside security to fully evaluate robustness against prompt injection attacks, as focusing solely on security masks the tradeoff involved.

Abstract: We identify a security-fidelity tradeoff in defending LLMs against indirect prompt injection: defenses resist injected instructions largely by suppressing untrusted text, which corrupts tasks that must preserve it, such as translation and document editing. Attack-success metrics cannot see this, because a model that ignores an injection and one that faithfully processes it as data score identically. We introduce SecFid, a benchmark built so that executing an injection, processing it as data, and ignoring it produce distinguishable outputs. This makes fidelity measurable and exposes a frontier: across 1,168 examples and 48 configurations, no model or defense achieves both objectives. The highest-fidelity model reaches 96.5% fidelity at 47.8% security, while the most secure defenses invert this, at 99.3% security but only 71.0%-73.9% fidelity. Even defenses with identical security differ in how they earn it: some repair hijacks into faithful processing, others simply suppress benign content. A decision-theoretic analysis shows why no fixed choice can be right everywhere: the correct behavior is not a property of the defense but of the deployment, set by its relative cost of a hijack versus a dropped span. Security alone therefore measures only half of robustness, and reporting it without fidelity hides the price at which it was bought.

</details>


### [567] [AI-Generated PowerShell Malware: An Experimental Framework and Dataset](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30819)
*Luciano Pianese,Vittorio Orbinato,Pietro Liguori,Roberto Natella*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper addresses the cybersecurity risks of generative AI by proposing a sandbox analysis framework for LLM-generated PowerShell malware and providing a new annotated dataset. It also evaluates open-weight LLMs, finding high similarity between real and generated malware.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and counteract the threats posed by malicious code generated by generative AI tools, especially through scripting languages like PowerShell.

Method: Introduced an experimental sandbox framework for dynamic analysis of AI-generated PowerShell malware. Developed and shared a manual dataset of real-world PowerShell malware annotated in natural language.

Result: The study showed strong resemblances between real-world malware and LLM-generated ones, with a median Jaccard index of 84.5% and nearly half achieving full overlap in malicious events triggered.

Conclusion: LLM-generated malware poses real cybersecurity threats due to its resemblance to real-world malware, emphasizing the need for frameworks to assess and mitigate these risks.

Abstract: Generative AI has emerged as a significant cybersecurity threat, with several recent attack campaigns leveraging LLMs to generate code for malicious purposes via scripting languages such as PowerShell. Consequently, for cybersecurity analysts, it is imperative to investigate the offensive capabilities of AI code generators. In this paper, we propose an experimental framework to assess LLM-generated PowerShell malware, which comprises a novel sandbox approach for dynamic analysis of AI-generated malware. Furthermore, we present a novel, manually curated dataset of real-world PowerShell malware, annotated in natural language to assist the training and evaluation of LLMs. Finally, this study evaluates permissive, open-weight LLMs adapted to PowerShell malware generation. Our results reveal a high degree of similarity between real malware and LLM-generated ones in terms of triggered OS malicious events, with a median Jaccard index of 84.5% and 48.4% of instances achieving complete overlap.

</details>


### [568] [Certified Speculative Execution for Untrusted AI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31023)
*Chenyu Zhou,Qiliang Jiang,Shuning Wu,Xu Zhou*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper proposes a system, Certificate-Gated Prefix Acceptance (CGPA), which ensures constraint safety, minimal regret, and improved computational speed for AI decision systems by combining untrusted proposals with trusted verifications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern AI lacks a certified way to reconcile test-time feasibility guarantees with computational speed in hard-constrained sequential decision systems, making it risky to rely on untrusted AI outputs directly.

Method: The authors introduce CGPA, a contract-based system involving: (1) a trusted verifier to reject constraint violations, (2) a calibrated value boundary to identify optimal low-cost execution prefixes, and (3) fallback to a safe solver for the rest. This approach decouples safety, regret, and speed.

Result: CGPA eliminates constraint violations, reduces regret drastically (within sampling noise of an oracle), and accelerates performance significantly. For example, it achieves a 2.96x speedup over traditional methods on a deployment-scale task with minimal regret (2.1%).

Conclusion: CGPA ensures safety, scales computational performance with the capability of the untrusted source, and establishes a reliable alternative for combining AI-led decision-making with hard-constrained systems.

Abstract: Hard-constrained sequential decision systems have no certified way to spend the test-time compute of modern AI: executing the multi-step drafts of a learned policy or a frozen LLM forfeits the feasibility guarantee a trusted solver provides, while invoking the solver at every step forfeits the speed the AI offers. Certificate-Gated Prefix Acceptance (CGPA) closes this gap with a certified speculative-execution contract for untrusted AI agents: a trusted verifier rejects constraint-violating transitions exactly, a conformally calibrated value boundary gates the longest low-cost prefix within a per-segment regret budget, and the rest defers to the solver, so safety, regret, and speed decouple by construction. The contract drives every untrusted proposal source - adversarial drafters and six heterogeneous frozen LLMs (including a 12B model that violates constraints in 98% of direct rollouts) - to zero applied violations; a certificate-aware learned boundary, conformally calibrated, drives mean regret three orders of magnitude below unguarded acceptance, to within sampling noise of the stepwise oracle (95% CI spanning zero), and under calendar shift a learned proposal source overtakes it on 15 of 18 held-out days. On a deployment-scale unit-commitment instance it turns a frozen 8B LLM into a 2.96x per-episode wall-clock speedup at 2.1% regret, outpacing the domain heuristic (1.79x) and a safe receding-horizon baseline (1.07x): the more capable the untrusted source, the faster the certified system, at guarantees that never change.

</details>


### [569] [Curvature-Guided Module Localization for Low-Rank Detoxification of Backdoored Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30899)
*Arash Raftari,Mehrdad Mahdavi,Nathan Blackthorn,Andrew Arash Mahyari*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper addresses backdoor attacks on large language models (LLMs) and proposes a post hoc mechanism for detoxification that targets weight-space repairs in localized modules instead of retraining the entire model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Backdoor attacks in LLMs can result in malicious behavior when hidden triggers are present, and there is a practical need to detoxify such models without retraining them from scratch.

Method: The framework involves identifying and localizing modules that propagate trigger-induced behavior using activation patching and Fisher/K-FAC curvature analysis, followed by low-rank targeted repair of the most influential modules.

Result: The method was evaluated on poisoned versions of Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct with various trigger positions, showing that the approach effectively suppresses malicious responses while preserving benign behavior.

Conclusion: Backdoor removal can be approached as a structural repair issue localized in relevant modules, offering an alternative to broad behavioral alignment.

Abstract: Backdoor attacks pose a serious threat to large language models (LLMs) by causing otherwise benign systems to produce attacker-specified malicious behavior when a hidden trigger is present. In this work, we study post hoc detoxification of backdoored LLMs in a practical setting where the defender has access to the poisoned model but does not wish to retrain the full network from scratch. We propose a mechanistically guided weight-space repair framework that first localizes modules involved in propagating trigger-induced behavior using activation patching and Fisher/K-FAC curvature analysis, and then applies targeted low-rank repair to only the most influential modules. We evaluate the method on poisoned variants of \texttt{Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct} with triggers inserted at the beginning, middle, and end of otherwise benign prompts. Results show that the proposed approach substantially suppresses trigger-conditioned malicious responses while preserving benign model behavior. These findings suggest that backdoor removal in LLMs can be formulated as a localized structural repair problem rather than only a broad behavioral alignment problem.

</details>


### [570] [Probe Choice Changes Canary-Memorization Verdicts: Three Post-Hoc Disagreement Case Studies in a Text-Dominant LoRA-Tuned Autoregressive Testbed](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31168)
*Zhichao Fan,Zexin Zhuang,Yanhang Li*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper audits a mean-NLL memorization probe on a Qwen2.5-VL-7B model and uncovers three cases of disagreement with other evaluation methods, suggesting further measures to improve secret specificity reporting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation stems from understanding and improving the reliability of a memorization probe (mean-NLL) in measuring model interactions with secret spans on controlled canary testbeds.

Method: The paper evaluates a fixed prefix-window mean-NLL memorization probe with a window size of K=20, uncovering discrepancies via cases such as false negatives, false positives, and ambiguous drops in performance.

Result: Three specific cases (C3, C4, C5) of probe disagreement with other metrics were identified, showing limitations in detecting or correctly attributing secret content interactions.

Conclusion: The study recommends using additional metrics like full-span secret NLL, span-localized decomposition, exact-recall, and decoy probes to strengthen and contextualize assertions about secret-specificity.

Abstract: We audit a fixed prefix-window mean-NLL memorization probe (K=20) on a Qwen2.5-VL-7B canary testbed and report three post-hoc cases where it disagrees with full-span secret NLL or greedy exact-recall. C3 (false negative, window truncation): damage lands on hex tokens outside K=20; the probe stays flat while hit@1 drops. C4 (false positive, non-secret drift): the probe moves, but approximately 99% sits on non-secret preamble; the secret span and hit@1 are unchanged. C5 (ambiguous in-window drop): the probe falls on an undertrained baseline while full-span hex is positive and hit@1=0. Recommendation: report (i) full-span secret NLL, (ii) a span-localised decomposition, (iii) behavioural exact-recall at k>=4, and (iv) decoy probes before asserting secret-specificity. Evidence is on controlled canaries in one backbone; magnitudes are testbed-specific.

</details>


### [571] [CSO-LLM: Class Subspace Orthogonalization for Post-Training Backdoor Detection and Trigger Inversion in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31309)
*Zhengxing Li,David J. Miller,Guangmingmei Yang,George Kesidis*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces a method for detecting and reversing backdoor attacks in large language models (LLMs), using a novel class subspace orthogonalization (CSO) approach.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of methods for detecting and mitigating backdoor attacks in LLMs compared to existing methods for image-based AIs.

Method: The authors implemented a detection framework leveraging CSO to enhance detection performance and penalize triggers that mimic target class signals. They propose both continuous optimization in token embedding space and greedy accretion in token space.

Result: The proposed methods demonstrate strong performance in detecting backdoor attacks and accurately identifying triggers across various LLM architectures and tasks.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively addresses challenges in LLM backdoor detection by introducing a method that enhances detection accuracy and improves trigger inversion.

Abstract: While post-training backdoor detection and trigger inversion schemes have been developed for AIs used e.g. for images, there is a paucity of such methods for LLMs. First, the LLM input space is discrete, with up to 150,000^k k-tuples to consider with k the token-length of a putative trigger. Second, one must blacklist tokens typical of the putative target response (class) of an attack, as such tokens may give false detection signals. However, a comprehensive blacklist is not available, in general, for a given domain. We develop a highly effective detection and inversion framework for LLMs treated as classifiers. Central to our approach is class subspace orthogonalization (CSO), a novel plug-and-play paradigm for backdoor detection that serves two fundamental roles when applied to LLMs: i) it enhances both sensitivity and specificity of a baseline detector; ii) it provides a form of implicit blacklisting, as it penalizes against inclusion, in a candidate trigger, of tokens that induce signal perturbations "in the direction of" the putative target class of an attack. One version of our detector performs continuous optimization in token embedding space, while a companion trigger-inversion and detection method performs greedy accretion in discrete token space. Our methods give both strong detection performance and accurate inversion of ground-truth triggers on several LLM classification domains, and for several different LLM architectures.

</details>


### [572] [CVE-TTP KG: Knowledge Graph Linking Software Vulnerabilities to Attack Behaviors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31557)
*Basant Agarwal,Dincy R. Arikkat,Swati Yadav,Serena Nicolazzo,Antonino Nocera,Vinod P*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper presents a CVE-TTP Knowledge Graph linking software vulnerabilities to behavioral patterns from the MITRE ATT&CK framework, using transformer models for behavior identification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional defenses are challenged by adversaries exploiting software vulnerabilities, and existing CVE and NVD databases lack connections to attacker behaviors, limiting threat response effectiveness.

Method: The work constructs a CVE-TTP Knowledge Graph through classification and relation extraction, leveraging transformer-based models like CySecBERT and developing annotated datasets for entity and relation extraction.

Result: CySecBERT achieved F1-scores of 87.71% for techniques and 96.16% for tactics. Annotated datasets had 24,820 entities and 43,608 relations, with the pipeline model achieving 0.86 and 0.99 F1-scores for entity and relation extraction respectively.

Conclusion: Integrating the outputs into a Neo4j-based Cyber Threat Knowledge Graph provides a structured visualization of vulnerabilities and bridges the gap between software vulnerabilities and attacker behaviors.

Abstract: In the evolving threat landscape, adversaries exploit software vulnerabilities to launch sophisticated attacks, challenging traditional defenses. Although databases like CVE and NVD provide detailed technical information, they often lack links to attacker behaviors such as tactics and techniques, limiting effective threat interpretation and response. This work bridges this gap by connecting vulnerabilities with behavioral patterns from the MITRE ATT&CK framework. We construct a CVE-TTP Knowledge Graph that links CVEs to tactics and techniques using classification and relation extraction. Transformer-based models are developed for behavior identification, with CySecBERT achieving macro F1-scores of 87.71% (techniques) and 96.16% (tactics). Also, we created an annotated dataset with 24,820 entities and 43,608 relations for entity and relation extraction. The pipeline-based approach achieves macro F1-scores of 0.86 (entity extraction) and 0.99 (relation extraction), while a span-based joint model achieves 0.78. These outputs are integrated into a Neo4j-based Cyber Threat Knowledge Graph, enabling structured visualization of vulnerabilities.

</details>


### [573] [Comparative Analysis of Machine Learning based Intrusion Detection in Realistic IoT Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31594)
*Rana Alharbi,Chuadhry Mujeeb Ahmed*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper analyzes IoT security, focusing on intrusion detection using the Gotham2025 dataset. It compares five machine learning algorithms and finds Random Forest as the top performer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper is motivated by the expanding role of IoT in critical sectors and the associated challenges in ensuring security and privacy due to IoT's resource limitations.

Method: The authors used the Gotham2025 dataset, which incorporates data from 78 emulated IoT devices utilizing MQTT, CoAP, and RTSP protocols. Five machine learning algorithms were compared for intrusion detection.

Result: The Random Forest Classifier achieved the highest F1-score of 0.99, indicating its high efficiency in classifying attacks.

Conclusion: Random Forest outperformed other algorithms for intrusion detection, showcasing its potential in enhancing IoT network security.

Abstract: The Internet of Things (IoT) is rapidly growing and expanding into various sectors, such as healthcare, transportation, smart homes, and more. Despite the benefits of using IoT devices, they present several challenges. Given the significant role these devices play in our lives, it is crucial to address issues related to their security and privacy. These devices are limited in resources, which complicates their security and the protection of the data that they manage. The paper aims to examine intrusion detection systems using the Gotham2025 dataset, generated through the Gotham testbed, which consists of 78 emulated IoT devices utilising various protocols, including MQTT, CoAP, and RTSP, to assist in safeguarding IoT networks from attacks. We conduct a comparative analysis between five machine learning algorithms, including Random Forest, XGBoost, Logistic Regression, Naive Bayes, and Deep Neural Network. We demonstrate that the Random Forest Classifier was the top-performing model, achieving an F1-score of 0.99 in classifying attacks.

</details>


### [574] [A Lifecycle and Application-Stack Survey of Large Language Model Vulnerabilities: Attacks, Risks, Defenses, and Open Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31639)
*Seyed Bagher Hashemi Natanzi,Bo Tang*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper systematizes vulnerabilities in large language model (LLM) systems by examining their lifecycle and application stack, analyzing attacks across eight stages, and proposing a research agenda for secure systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address security risks that arise not only from LLMs’ model weights but also their applications and interactions with broader systems.

Method: The authors classify vulnerabilities across eight lifecycle stages of LLM systems, analyze associated risks and defenses, and propose a comprehensive framework for understanding these vulnerabilities.

Result: The study highlights trust boundary failures, how untrusted data can become executable instructions, and why isolated defenses are insufficient for securing LLM systems.

Conclusion: The paper underscores the need for compositional security and proposes a research agenda to develop more secure LLM systems across their application landscape.

Abstract: Large language models are no longer only text generators. They are increasingly embedded in retrieval pipelines, enterprise assistants, coding environments, robotic systems, security-operation workflows, and autonomous agents that can read private data, call tools, write files, execute code, and act across organizational boundaries. This shift changes the security problem: risks do not arise from the model weights alone, but from the full lifecycle and application stack through which data, prompts, model outputs, tools, memories, and user authority interact. This paper systematizes the literature on vulnerabilities in large language model systems through a lifecycle and application-stack lens. We organize attacks across eight stages: data collection, pretraining, post-training alignment, model packaging and supply chain, retrieval and memory, prompting and inference, tool/agent execution, and deployment/maintenance. For each stage, we analyze attacker capabilities, affected security objectives, representative attacks, practical risks, evaluation practices, and defenses. We further map LLM-specific vulnerabilities to confidentiality, integrity, availability, safety, privacy, fairness, accountability, and agency-control objectives. Unlike taxonomies that list isolated attack names, the proposed systematization emphasizes where trust boundaries fail, how untrusted data becomes executable instruction, how delegated authority amplifies model errors, and why point defenses rarely compose. We close with a research agenda for secure LLM systems, including compositional security, provenance-aware retrieval, tool-call containment, long-horizon agent evaluation, privacy-preserving adaptation, realistic red teaming, and deployment-grade incident response.

</details>


<div id='q-bio.QM'></div>

# q-bio.QM [[Back]](#toc)

### [575] [Modeling Cell-Cycle-Aware Single-Cell Drug Perturbation Responses](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30695)
*Dingping Zhao,Jie Lin*

Main category: q-bio.QM

TL;DR: The paper introduces scCycleMol, a framework for cell-cycle-aware prediction of single-cell drug perturbations, addressing the challenge of integrating proliferative state changes in perturbation modeling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods treat cell-cycle variation as nuisance rather than valuable information, and drug-induced phase changes are rarely a primary focus in prediction models.

Method: The authors present scCycleMol, which utilizes a SciPlex3 benchmark dataset with cell-cycle supervision, and implements a learnable full-expression cell-cycle head for circular phase prediction. Different pretraining strategies and molecular representations are evaluated.

Result: scCycleMol demonstrates improved out-of-distribution expression prediction, achieving higher r squared scores than baselines (e.g., 0.9093 vs. 0.6800) for expected all-gene expressions. Its cell-cycle supervision enhances phase accuracy by 0.5–0.6 points without compromising expression prediction.

Conclusion: Including explicit cell-cycle-aware supervision significantly enhances the performance and phase accuracy of perturbation models, offering a robust framework for transcriptional drug-response prediction.

Abstract: Single-cell drug perturbation models should predict not only transcriptional response magnitude, but also whether a treatment alters the proliferative state of a cell. This is challenging because cell-cycle variation is often treated as nuisance variation, and benchmark pipelines rarely treat drug-induced phase changes as a primary prediction target. We introduce scCycleMol, a cell-cycle-aware perturbation prediction framework built on a curated 24-hour SciPlex3 benchmark with standardized molecule identities, dose and cell-line metadata, and gene expression with cell-cycle supervision derived from treated states. Instead of using cell-cycle state as an input covariate, scCycleMol derives supervision from predicted treated expression and propagates it through a learnable full-expression cell-cycle head with circular G1/S/G2M phase targets. We evaluate marker-based supervision, molecular representations, and pretraining strategies to isolate sources of improvement. Across a SciPlex3 benchmark with over 600k cells, 186 perturbation conditions, multiple cancer cell lines, and thousands of genes, scCycleMol improves out-of-distribution expression prediction compared with conditional perturbation baselines. The best LINCS-pretrained circular model achieves 0.9093 expected all-gene r squared and 0.6843 expected differentially expressed gene r squared, compared with 0.6800 and 0.5400 for LINCS-pretrained ChemCPA. Closed-loop cell-cycle supervision improves phase accuracy by about 0.5 to 0.6 points while maintaining nearly unchanged expression prediction. A Tahoe-pretrained variant reaches 0.9609 phase accuracy, highlighting the benefit of explicit cell-cycle-aware supervision in perturbation modeling.

</details>
